title: From BI to AI
text:
Preface
Traditional business intelligence — dashboards, static reports, and fixed queries — is being replaced by AI agents that understand your questions, query your data, and deliver answers in seconds. This course gives professionals from any background a hands-on understanding of how agentic AI transforms analysis, reporting, and decision-making. No coding experience is required.
Module 1 explains what AI agents are and why they are displacing dashboards as the primary interface between decision-makers and data. It lays out the conceptual foundation — the agent loop, tool use, and the shift from fixed reports to conversational intelligence — that the rest of the course builds on.
Module 2 shows how to query data and generate visualizations and reports with plain English. You will open a coding agent in a folder containing a spreadsheet and go from raw data to a written recommendation with charts in under ten minutes.
Module 3 shows how AI can help you think through decisions. You will use AI as a sparring partner — challenging assumptions, running pre-mortems, and stress-testing your own analysis from Module 2.
Module 4 shows how AI can be connected to your company’s databases, so anyone with proper credentials can query them in plain English. You will learn about MCP connectors, build a reusable “skill” that encodes a database schema, and build a demo web app that anyone can use from a browser.
Module 5 addresses reliability and compliance. You will validate AI outputs, red-team skills, and build the governance framework your organization needs before deploying any of this.
Module 6 shows how to automate a reporting pipeline. You will build an agent workflow that queries a database, generates charts, writes summaries, and assembles a PowerPoint deck — all from a single instruction.
Module 7 explores agent workflows beyond reporting — email agents, calendar agents, scheduling as conversational AI, document processing agents that ingest multiple file formats (Excel, PDF, Word, CSV) and combine the results into a single analysis, and variance analysis agents that decompose budget misses into actionable drivers.
Module 8 shows how to use AI to query your company’s documents and return answers grounded in your documents (with citations) rather than relying on its general knowledge. This is called Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG).

title: From BI to AI
text:
Data files
The exercises in this course use three datasets.
The Superstore CSV contains roughly 10,000 retail orders across four U.S. regions with columns for sales, profit, discount, product category, and customer segment.1 It is used throughout Modules 2, 3, and 5.
The Employee Attrition CSV contains 1,470 employee records with 35 attributes including department, job role, monthly income, overtime status, job satisfaction, and whether the employee left the company.2 It is used in Module 2 exercises.
The Chinook database is a SQLite file representing a music retailer’s operations with 11 related tables covering employees, customers, invoices, tracks, albums, artists, and genres.3 It is the primary dataset for Module 4 and is revisited in Modules 5 and 6.
Module 8 uses a sample company handbook for RAG exercises.

title: From BI to AI
text:
Prerequisites
No prior coding or data science experience is assumed. The AI writes the code. You provide the questions, the judgment, and the domain expertise.
You need Python and the Claude Desktop app installed before the first class session. We provide installers that handle everything:
Windows — download the Windows installer. Right-click INSTALL.bat and select Run as administrator. The script installs Python 3.12, Claude Desktop, and the skills used in this course.
Mac — download the Mac installer. Open Terminal and run:
chmod +x ~/Downloads/install.sh
~/Downloads/install.sh
The script installs Homebrew (if needed), Python 3.12, Claude Desktop, and skills.
After either installer finishes, open Claude Desktop and sign in with your Anthropic account. Claude Code requires either a Pro subscription at $20 per month (which includes a generous usage allowance) or an Anthropic API key with credits.






Installing manually





If the installer does not work for Python, download Python 3.10 or later from python.org. On Windows, check “Add Python to PATH” during installation — this is in small print on the bottom of the very first installation screen. On Mac, you can also install via Homebrew with brew install python. Verify by opening a terminal and running python --version.
If the installer does not work for Claude Desktop, download it directly from claude.com/download and run the installer.









Verify your setup





Open Claude Desktop, click the Code tab, select a folder, and ask:

Create a CSV file with five rows of sample sales data — columns for date, product, revenue, and profit. Then describe the data back to me.

If the agent creates the file and describes it, you are ready for class.

title: 1  AI Agents
text:
1.1 The evolution of the user interface
Something big is happening in how organizations use data. For decades, business intelligence meant dashboards — fixed views of fixed data, built by specialists, delivered weeks after the question was asked. That era is ending. AI agents are replacing dashboards with a fundamentally different model: you ask a question in plain English, and an intelligent system queries your data, runs the analysis, and delivers the answer in seconds.
This module explains what AI agents are, why they are displacing traditional BI, and what the shift means for anyone who makes decisions with data. The rest of the course shows you how to do it.
The way humans interact with computers has changed in waves. Each wave made technology accessible to a larger audience by removing a layer of technical skill.
Command-line interfaces required users to memorize syntax. Graphical interfaces replaced syntax with point-and-click. Dashboards replaced raw queries with pre-built visualizations. Now, natural language is replacing all of them. You describe what you want in ordinary English, and the AI figures out how to get it.
Each transition expanded who could use computers productively. The current transition — from dashboards to conversational AI — is the most consequential for business, because it removes the last barrier between a decision-maker and the data they need.

title: 1  AI Agents
text:
1.2 What is an AI agent?
To understand why agents are replacing dashboards, you first need to understand what an agent is and how it differs from a chatbot.
A chatbot is a relay. You type a question, the chatbot sends it to a large language model (LLM), and the LLM sends back a text reply. The conversation is purely verbal — the chatbot cannot take action, look things up, or run code. It can only talk.
An agent adds tools. Like a chatbot, it communicates with an LLM. But the agent also has an execution layer — it can run code, read and write files, query databases, call APIs, and take action in the real world. The LLM decides what to do; the execution layer does it.






flowchart LR
    subgraph Chatbot["Chatbot"]
        direction LR
        U1["User"] -- "question" --> CB["Chatbot"] -- "prompt +\nhistory" --> LLM1["LLM"]
        LLM1 -- "text reply" --> CB -- "answer" --> U1
    end

    subgraph Agent["Coding Agent"]
        direction LR
        U2["User"] -- "question" --> AG["Agent"] -- "prompt +\nhistory" --> LLM2["LLM"]
        LLM2 -- "text or\ntool call" --> AG
        AG <-- "run code,\nread files" --> Tools["Tools"]
        AG -- "answer +\nartifacts" --> U2
    end

    style Chatbot fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#2196f3
    style Agent fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#4caf50




Figure 1.2: A chatbot relays messages to the LLM. An agent also has tools — it can run code, read files, and take action.





This distinction matters enormously. A chatbot can describe how to analyze your sales data. An agent does the analysis — it reads the spreadsheet, writes Python, computes the metrics, renders the charts, and writes the memo. You get results, not instructions.

title: 1  AI Agents
section: 1.3 How agents work: the agent loop
text:
1.3 How agents work: the agent loop
Every agent follows a loop: plan, execute, observe, iterate.

Plan — the LLM reads your request and breaks it into steps.
Execute — the agent calls a tool (runs code, queries a database, writes a file).
Observe — the agent checks the result of the action.
Iterate — if the result is not satisfactory, the agent adjusts its plan and tries again.

The loop repeats until the task is complete. Multiple rounds of communication occur between the LLM and the tools before the agent returns output to you.






flowchart TB
    Plan["Plan\nBreak task into steps"]
    Execute["Execute\nRun code, call tools"]
    Observe["Observe\nCheck results"]
    Iterate["Iterate\nAdjust the plan"]

    Plan --> Execute --> Observe --> Iterate --> Plan

    style Plan fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#2196f3
    style Execute fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#ff9800
    style Observe fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#4caf50
    style Iterate fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#9c27b0




Figure 1.3: The agent loop — plan, execute, observe, iterate — repeats until the task is done.





When an agent encounters an error — a SQL query that returns no results, a chart that renders incorrectly, a file that does not exist — it does not stop and ask you what to do. It diagnoses the problem, revises its approach, and tries again. This autonomous problem-solving is what separates agents from simple automation scripts.

Tools an agent can use
The LLM does not run code or call databases directly. When it decides a tool is needed, it returns a structured message — something like {"tool": "sql_query", "query": "SELECT ..."}. The agent’s execution layer reads that message, runs the actual tool, and sends the result back to the LLM. The LLM reads the result and decides what to do next.
Common agent tools include:

Python environment — for data analysis, charting, and computation
Database connections — for querying SQL databases
File system — for reading, writing, and editing files
Web search and APIs — for fetching external data
Document creation — for producing Excel, Word, and PowerPoint files

An agent can use multiple tools in sequence. For example: query a database, pass the results to Python for analysis, generate a chart, write a narrative summary, and assemble everything into a PowerPoint deck — all from a single instruction.

title: 1  AI Agents
text:
1.4 Levels of agent capability
Not all agents are equally powerful. The tools an agent can access determine what it can do, and the difference between levels is dramatic.

Agents with code execution
Web-based AI assistants like Claude.ai and ChatGPT can write and run code in a sandboxed environment. This is a major step beyond a chatbot. You can upload a spreadsheet and say “analyze this data” — the agent writes Python, executes it, and returns charts and tables.
But the sandbox has walls. The agent cannot install new software, connect to your company’s database, read files from your laptop, or save results anywhere permanent. Every conversation starts from scratch. It is powerful for one-off analysis, but it cannot build anything that persists.


Agents with terminal access
A terminal agent like Claude Code operates directly on your computer through the command line. It can do everything a code-execution agent can do, plus everything you could do yourself at a terminal:

Install software and packages — set up Python libraries, database drivers, or any tool the task requires.
Read and write files anywhere — access spreadsheets on your desktop, edit configuration files, create entire project directories.
Connect to databases — query your company’s SQL Server, PostgreSQL, or SQLite databases directly.
Use git and version control — create branches, commit changes, push to GitHub.
Build and run applications — create a web app, start a local server, and open it in your browser.
Chain external tools — call APIs, run shell scripts, orchestrate multi-step workflows across different programs.
Deploy to production — push a finished application to a hosting service so others can use it.

The difference is not incremental — it is qualitative. A code-execution agent can analyze your sales data. A terminal agent can analyze your sales data, build a web dashboard from the results, connect it to your live database so it updates automatically, and deploy it so your entire team can access it — all from a single conversation.






Example: from question to deployed app



In a single session with a terminal agent, you might say:

“Connect to our sales database and show me revenue by region for 2025.”
“Build a Streamlit app that lets anyone on the team explore this data with filters.”
“Deploy it so the team can access it at a URL.”

Each instruction triggers dozens of tool calls — writing Python files, installing packages, running the app locally to test it, fixing bugs, and finally deploying. What would take a developer a day takes the agent minutes.


This course uses Claude Code — a terminal agent — because it can do everything the web-based tools can do and far more. When you need to connect to a database, build an application, or produce a deliverable that lives beyond a chat window, terminal access is what makes it possible.






Where does the code run?





AI can execute code in three environments, each with different trade-offs.
A cloud sandbox is a temporary environment hosted by the AI provider. Claude.ai and ChatGPT offer this: you upload a file, the AI processes it on a remote server, and you download the results. Convenient for quick analysis, but your data leaves your machine and the session is ephemeral.
A virtual machine runs on your own computer in an isolated sandbox. Claude Desktop’s Cowork tab is an example: it spins up a VM locally, giving AI a full computing environment with persistence across sessions. Your data stays on your machine.
A local environment runs directly on your machine with no isolation. Claude Desktop’s Code tab does this: the agent writes code that executes right where you work, with full access to your files, databases, and network. Nothing is uploaded. This is what we use throughout this course.





flowchart LR
    subgraph Cloud["Cloud Sandbox"]
        direction TB
        C1["Code runs on\nprovider's servers"]
        C2["Data uploaded\ntemporarily"]
        C3["Data leaves\nyour machine"]
    end
    subgraph VM["Virtual Machine"]
        direction TB
        V1["Code runs in\nlocal VM"]
        V2["Files accessible\nwithin VM"]
        V3["Data stays\non your machine"]
    end
    subgraph Local["Local App"]
        direction TB
        L1["Code runs\ndirectly on your machine"]
        L2["Full access to\nfiles & databases"]
        L3["Nothing\nuploaded"]
    end
    Cloud --> VM --> Local


 Three code execution environments, from most isolated to most integrated.

title: 1  AI Agents
text:
1.5 The dashboard trap
Organizations spend millions building dashboards. When a new question arises, the cycle restarts.

A business user requests a report.
A team builds the queries, charts, and filters and deploys them.
The user asks a follow-up — back to step 1.

The cost is measured in three dimensions. Time: weeks to months per dashboard. Money: BI licenses, engineering hours, maintenance. Rigidity: fixed views of fixed data that cannot adapt to a new question.
According to Gartner, fewer than half of data and analytics teams effectively provide value to their organizations. The reason is not lack of data — it is that dashboards answer yesterday’s questions. The most valuable analysis comes from ad-hoc questions that arise in the moment:

“What happened to margins in the Southeast last quarter?”
“Show me our top 10 customers by growth rate, excluding one-time orders.”
“Compare Q3 headcount vs. budget by department, and flag anyone over 110%.”

These are simple questions. Getting answers should not require a development cycle.

title: 1  AI Agents
section: 1.6 Natural language as the query interface
text:
1.6 Natural language as the query interface
The dashboard was a workaround for the fact that databases do not speak English. Now they do.
With a natural-language AI agent, you skip the entire dashboard build cycle and go straight from question to answer.









Traditional Dashboard
AI Agent




How you ask
Click filters, select dates
Type in plain English


Response time
Minutes to weeks
Seconds


Follow-ups
New dashboard request
Next sentence


What you need to know
Dashboard layout, filter options
What questions to ask



Consider what this looks like in practice. You say: “Show me monthly revenue by product line for 2025.” The agent writes SQL, queries the database, and produces a grouped bar chart. You say: “Break out Enterprise by region.” The agent refines the query and updates the chart. Behind the scenes, four different SQL queries were written and three visualizations produced. You needed to know nothing except what questions to ask.

title: 1  AI Agents
text:
1.7 The database agent pattern
The most powerful dashboard replacement is an AI agent connected to your database.

Connect the database (via a schema description, an API, or a protocol like MCP).
Give the AI the schema: table names, columns, relationships, and business context.
Start asking questions.

The agent writes and executes SQL queries, computes derived metrics (growth rates, ratios, variance decompositions), and generates charts — all from plain English. It can export results to Excel or assemble a PowerPoint deck.
Each of these tasks would take a BI team days to build as a dashboard. With a database agent, they take seconds.

title: 1  AI Agents
text:
1.8 Case study: HPE’s “Alfred”
The shift from dashboards to agents is not theoretical. It is already happening at scale.
Marie Myers, CFO of Hewlett Packard Enterprise (#143 on Fortune 500), described the transformation in Fortune (February 2026). HPE’s weekly operational review required 100+ slides and hundreds of hours of preparation across business units. There was no time left for forward-looking analysis.
HPE built “Alfred” — AI agents that pull, reconcile, and analyze data automatically. The result: 90% of manual preparation eliminated, cycle time reduced 40%, costs down 25%. Three thousand finance employees are being reskilled to build their own agents.
“The goal is for finance professionals to become masters of their own destiny rather than casualties of automation,” Myers said.
Derek Waldron, JP Morgan’s Chief Analytics Officer, described the same trajectory: workers shift from being creators of reports to checkers or managers of AI agents doing that work. The Deloitte Q4 2025 CFO Signals survey of 200 CFOs at $1B+ companies found that 87% say AI will be extremely or very important to finance operations in 2026, and 54% prioritize integrating AI agents in their finance departments.

title: 1  AI Agents
section: 1.9 What agents replace — and what they don’t
text:
1.9 What agents replace — and what they don’t
AI agents replace the mechanical work of pulling data, building charts, formatting slides, and writing boilerplate narrative. They do not replace judgment, domain expertise, or accountability.
A weekly ops review that took a data team 6+ hours to prepare and a manager 3 hours to review can be generated by an agent pipeline in under a minute. But someone still needs to interpret the results, decide what to act on, and present to stakeholders.
The agent does not replace the analyst’s judgment — it replaces the grunt work that keeps analysts from exercising their judgment.

title: 1  AI Agents
text:
1.10 The reporting pipeline
When an agent chains multiple steps together, the result is a reporting pipeline. A typical pipeline:

Query the database — pull the relevant data.
Transform and compute — calculate metrics, growth rates, variances.
Generate charts — render visualizations from the data.
Write narrative — generate an executive summary grounded in the numbers.
Assemble output — produce a PowerPoint deck, Word document, or web page.







flowchart LR
    Q["Query DB\n_DataFrame_"]
    C["Render Chart\n_PNG image_"]
    N["Write Narrative\n_summary_"]
    P["Assemble Deck\n_.pptx file_"]
    D["Deliver\n_screen + download_"]

    Q --> C --> N --> P --> D

    style Q fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#2196f3
    style C fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#4caf50
    style N fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#ff9800
    style P fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#9c27b0
    style D fill:#fce4ec,stroke:#e91e63




Figure 1.4: From raw data to polished report — no copy-paste, no formatting, no manual writing.





You do not need to build this by hand. You describe what you want to a coding agent, and it builds the pipeline for you. Your job is to test the result, refine the prompt, and iterate until the output meets your standards. Modules 3 through 5 show how.

title: 1  AI Agents
text:
1.11 What this course covers
This course is a hands-on guide to building the capabilities just described. Each module adds a layer of skill, from basic data analysis to production-ready AI systems.
Module 2: Exploring Data goes from a raw spreadsheet to a written recommendation with charts in under ten minutes — using nothing but plain-English conversation.
Module 3: AI as Thinking Partner shows how to use AI as a sparring partner for decisions — challenging assumptions, running pre-mortems, and stress-testing your own analysis.
Module 4: Connecting Databases shows how to connect AI to your company’s databases so anyone can query them in plain English. You will build a reusable skill and a demo web app.
Module 5: Reliability and Compliance provides the verification protocols, governance frameworks, and deployment patterns your organization needs before any of this goes to production.
Module 6: Automating with Agents chains database queries, charts, narratives, and document assembly into reporting pipelines that run from a single instruction.
Module 7: Agent Workflows explores agent workflows beyond reporting — document processing, variance analysis, email and calendar agents.
Module 8: Retrieval-Augmented Generation shows how to give AI knowledge of your company’s documents — policies, contracts, manuals — and get answers grounded in those documents with citations.
No coding experience is required. The AI writes the code. You provide the questions, the judgment, and the domain expertise.

title: 1  AI Agents
text:
1.12 Exercises






Exercise 1: Dashboard Inventory





List three dashboards or recurring reports your team uses. For each one, answer: How long does it take to produce? How often is it updated? When was the last time someone asked a question the dashboard could not answer?
Identify the one that would benefit most from being replaced by a natural-language agent. Write one paragraph explaining why.









Exercise 2: Agent vs. Dashboard Comparison





Pick one of the dashboards from Exercise 1. Write down three questions you would ask if you could query the underlying data in plain English — questions the dashboard cannot currently answer.
Then write down the steps a human analyst would take to answer each question: which data sources to pull, what joins or calculations are needed, and how to present the result. This is the work an agent would automate.









Exercise 3: The AI Landscape





Using Claude or another AI, research the current state of AI adoption in your industry. Ask:

What companies in your industry have deployed AI agents for data analysis or reporting?
What use cases are most common?
What barriers to adoption are most frequently cited?

Write a one-page summary of the landscape and identify the three most promising use cases for your organization.

title: 2  Exploring Data with AI
text:
2.1 Using the Code tab
Module 1 described how agents replace dashboards by letting you query data in plain English. This module puts that into practice. You will go from a raw spreadsheet to a written recommendation with charts in under ten minutes — using nothing but plain-English conversation. The agent reads your data, writes and runs Python, renders charts, and synthesizes findings — the full agent loop in action.
The Claude Desktop app has three tabs in the sidebar: Chat, Cowork, and Code. Click the Code tab. This is where you will do all of the work in this course.
The Code tab runs the Claude Code engine — a coding agent with full access to your local files, databases, and network. This is the agent architecture described in Module 1: an LLM connected to tools through an execution layer, running the plan-execute-observe-iterate loop autonomously. It reads files, writes and runs Python, renders charts, and produces documents, all from plain-English conversation.
To get started, click Code in the sidebar, then select a folder as your working directory. Choose a model (Sonnet is fast and capable; Opus is more thorough) and a permission mode. For the exercises in this course, “auto-accept” is the most convenient permission mode — it lets the agent run code without asking for approval on every step.
Claude Code reads a file called CLAUDE.md in the project directory for persistent instructions. This is where you store context about your data, preferred output formats, and any domain-specific guidance the agent should follow every session.
Installing Python gives you the base language, but most work relies on additional libraries — code packages for data analysis, charting, web apps, and more. At various points in this course, we will ask you to tell the coding agent to install specific libraries. If you skip that step, the agent will notice when it tries to run code that requires them and will ask you for permission to install what it needs. Just grant permission if that happens.

title: 2  Exploring Data with AI
text:
2.2 The scenario
You are a regional VP at a retail company. Q4 numbers just came in. The question on the table: which regions should get additional marketing budget, and which product lines should be expanded or cut?
The data is the Kaggle “Sample Superstore” dataset — roughly 10,000 orders across four U.S. regions, with columns for sales, profit, discount, product category, and customer segment. You can download it here: superstore.csv.
An analyst would take half a day to work through this. With a coding agent, you can do it in ten minutes.

title: 2  Exploring Data with AI
text:
2.3 Step 1: Open and orient
Place the CSV file in a folder and open your coding agent in that folder. Then ask:

Describe the data in this folder.

The agent reads the CSV into a dataframe and reports back: 10,000 rows, 21 columns, four regions, three product categories, a date range from 2014 to 2017, total sales of roughly $2.3 million with an average profit margin around 12%. It may also flag surprises — sub-categories that are losing money, or a disproportionate concentration of sales in one region.
This orienting step catches formatting issues (dates parsed as strings, currencies with dollar signs, encoding problems) and surfaces the structure of the data before you start asking pointed questions.

title: 2  Exploring Data with AI
text:
2.4 Step 2: Ask decision-relevant questions
Frame every question around the decision, not around the data. For the budget decision:

Which region has the highest profit margin, and which has the lowest?


Show quarterly sales trends by region. Are any accelerating or declining?


Which sub-categories are losing money? How much?

Each of these questions connects directly to the budget allocation decision. The first identifies where margin is strongest. The second reveals momentum — a region with declining sales may not deserve more investment regardless of its current margin. The third pinpoints money-losers that could be cut.

title: 2  Exploring Data with AI
text:
2.5 Step 3: Visualize the tradeoffs
Ask the agent to create charts that make the decision visual.
“Quarterly profit by region” calls for a line chart, because you are looking at trends over time. “Profit by sub-category, sorted” calls for a bar chart, because you are comparing categories. “Discount rate vs. profit margin” calls for a scatter plot, because you are looking for a relationship between two continuous variables.
Once you have a chart, iterate. Ask the agent to highlight money-losers in red, add a break-even line, or annotate the December spike. Each follow-up takes seconds. If an annotation overlaps a data point, you can ask the agent to view its own chart and fix the layout — it can see images and adjust the code accordingly.

title: 2  Exploring Data with AI
text:
2.6 Step 4: Build the recommendation
The final step is synthesis. Ask the agent to turn the analysis into a recommendation.

Which two regions should get increased marketing budget and why?


Which sub-categories should we discontinue? Quantify the savings.


Write a one-page executive memo with the recommendation and supporting charts.

In ten minutes you have gone from a raw CSV to a written recommendation backed by evidence. No code was written by you. No analyst was involved. No one waited three days for a dashboard.

title: 2  Exploring Data with AI
text:
2.7 What just happened
Behind the scenes, the agent read the CSV into Python, wrote and ran code for each question, rendered charts with Python, and synthesized findings into a memo. You never see the code unless you ask for it.

Figure 2.1: The four-step analysis workflow — you provide direction, the agent does the work.

title: 2  Exploring Data with AI
text:
2.8 Working with multiple files
The Superstore walkthrough used a single CSV. Real decisions rarely come from a single file. A more common scenario: five regional managers each submit a Q1 sales spreadsheet, and you need a consolidated view.
Download aggregation.zip and extract it into a folder. You will find five Excel files — one per region — each with columns for date, product, units sold, revenue, and cost.
Open your coding agent in that folder and start the same way:

Describe the data in this folder.

The agent reads all five files, reports the column names and row counts for each, and flags any inconsistencies (a column named “Revenue” in one file and “Total Revenue” in another, or a date formatted as text). It standardizes the columns and merges everything into a single dataset without you writing a line of code.
Now ask cross-file questions:

Which region had the highest total revenue in Q1?


Compare profit margins across regions.


Show monthly revenue trends by region as a line chart.

Every follow-up works across the combined dataset. You can ask for comparisons, rankings, and charts exactly as you did with the single Superstore file. The only difference is the starting point: instead of one file, you pointed the agent at a folder of files.
This pattern scales. Whether it is five files or fifty, the workflow is the same: place the files in a folder, let the agent orient itself, then ask your questions.

title: 2  Exploring Data with AI
text:
2.9 Combining different file types
The next step up is working across file formats. Consider an invoice reconciliation scenario: your accounting team has an Excel spreadsheet of payments, a PDF of vendor invoices, and a Word document summarizing disputed charges.
Download invoice-reconciliation.zip and extract it into a folder. You will find three files:

payments.xlsx — a spreadsheet listing every payment made this quarter, with vendor name, amount, date, and reference number
invoices.pdf — a set of vendor invoices with invoice numbers, dates, amounts, and descriptions
disputes.docx — a memo from the finance team flagging specific invoices that are under dispute, with reasons

Open your coding agent in that folder and ask:

Read all files in this folder and summarize what each one contains.

The agent reads Excel, PDF, and Word natively. It will describe the structure of each file — rows and columns for the spreadsheet, extracted tables and text from the PDF, and the narrative content of the Word document.
Now ask it to reconcile:

Match each invoice to its corresponding payment. Flag any invoices that have no matching payment and any payments that don’t match an invoice.

This is where the real value appears. The agent handles the messy matching — vendor names that are slightly different between files, date formats that don’t align, reference numbers in one file that correspond to invoice numbers in another. It produces a reconciliation table showing matched records, unmatched invoices, and unmatched payments.
Then layer in the dispute information:

Which of the unmatched invoices are under dispute according to the memo? Produce a final reconciliation summary.

The agent cross-references the Word document’s dispute notes with the unmatched items and produces a clean summary: invoices paid, invoices unpaid but not disputed, invoices under active dispute, and payments with no matching invoice.
This pattern — extract, match, reconcile — is the foundation of due diligence reviews, compliance audits, and month-end close processes. The file formats vary, but the workflow is the same: point the agent at the files, ask it to read and reconcile, then iterate until the summary is complete.

title: 2  Exploring Data with AI
text:
2.10 Exercises






Exercise 1: The Superstore Walkthrough





This is the core exercise for Module 2. Download superstore.csv and place it in a folder. Open your coding agent in that folder and work through the four steps described above.
First, ask the agent to describe the data. Confirm the row count, column names, date range, and financial summary. Second, ask three decision-relevant questions tied to the marketing budget allocation decision. Third, create at least one line chart (for trends), one bar chart (for comparisons), and one scatter plot (for relationships). Iterate on at least one chart by adding annotations or improving the layout. Fourth, ask the agent to write a one-page executive memo with the recommendation and supporting charts.
Bring your dataset and memo to Module 3 — you will use them as thinking-partner exercises.









Exercise 2: Data Detective





Your colleague has made several claims about the Superstore data. Use your coding agent to verify or debunk each one.
Claim 1: Discounts over 30% always increase total profit. Claim 2: The East region has the best profit margins. Claim 3: Office Supplies is the most profitable category. Claim 4: Q4 is always the best quarter.
For each claim, ask the agent to test it, show the evidence, and write one sentence stating whether the claim is confirmed or debunked, with the supporting number.









Exercise 3: Employee Attrition Walkthrough





You are an HR director at a mid-size company. Employee turnover has been rising and the CEO wants to know why people are leaving and what to do about it.
Download employee-attrition.csv and place it in a folder. Open your coding agent and work through the same four steps from this module.
First, ask the agent to describe the data. How many employees are in the dataset? What is the overall attrition rate? What departments and job roles are represented?
Second, ask decision-relevant questions: Which departments have the highest attrition? Does overtime correlate with leaving? How does monthly income differ between employees who stay and those who leave? Are employees with longer commutes more likely to leave?
Third, create at least three charts: a bar chart comparing attrition rates by department, a box plot of monthly income for stayers vs. leavers, and a chart of your choice that reveals a pattern you find interesting.
Fourth, ask the agent to write a one-page memo recommending three retention interventions, backed by the data.









Exercise 4: Attrition Data Detective





Your VP of People Operations has made several claims about the employee attrition data. Use your coding agent to verify or debunk each one.
Claim 1: Employees who work overtime leave at more than double the rate of those who don’t. Claim 2: Job satisfaction has no meaningful relationship with attrition. Claim 3: Younger employees (under 30) are the most likely to leave. Claim 4: Employees who haven’t been promoted in the last two years leave at higher rates.
For each claim, ask the agent to test it, show the evidence, and write one sentence stating whether the claim is confirmed or debunked, with the supporting number.









Exercise 5: The Weekly Report





Identify a recurring report your team produces — weekly sales, monthly KPIs, quarterly review — and try to automate it.
Place the source data in a folder and open your coding agent. Ask it to produce the full report: charts, tables, and a narrative summary. Then compare: how long does this report normally take to produce? How long did AI take? What is missing from the AI version?









Exercise 6: Regional Sales Consolidation





Download aggregation.zip and extract it into a folder. You will find five Excel files, one for each region’s Q1 sales data.
Open your coding agent in the folder and work through these steps:

Ask the agent to read all files and describe the data. Confirm the column names, row counts, and any inconsistencies it finds.
Ask: which region had the highest total revenue? Which had the best profit margin? Are there products that sell well in one region but poorly in another?
Create at least two charts: a bar chart comparing total revenue by region and a chart of your choice that reveals a cross-region pattern.
Ask the agent to write a short recommendation: based on Q1 results, where should the company focus Q2 marketing spend, and why?










Exercise 7: Invoice Reconciliation





Download invoice-reconciliation.zip and extract it into a folder. You will find three files: a payments spreadsheet (Excel), a set of vendor invoices (PDF), and a dispute memo (Word).
Open your coding agent in the folder and work through these steps:

Ask the agent to read all three files and summarize each one.
Ask it to match invoices to payments and flag any that don’t match.
Ask it to cross-reference unmatched invoices with the dispute memo.
Ask for a final reconciliation summary: invoices paid, invoices unpaid but not disputed, invoices under dispute, and payments with no matching invoice.

When you are done, you should have a clean reconciliation table that an accountant could use to close out the quarter.









Exercise 8: Instruction Files





Create a CLAUDE.md file in a folder containing the sample CSV from Exercise 1. Add two instructions: a preferred chart style (for example, “use a dark background with white text for all charts”) and a default analysis behavior (for example, “always show the code you run”).
Open the Code tab in that folder and ask the agent to create a chart. Does it follow your instructions? Modify the instruction file and ask again.

title: 3  AI as Thinking Partner
text:
3.1 Why AI is a good sparring partner
Module 2 showed how AI can analyze data in minutes. But many of the hardest decisions in business are not data problems — they are thinking problems. The data may be clear, but the implications are not. The options are not fully explored. The assumptions are unchallenged. The failure modes are unexamined.
This module is about using AI as a sparring partner: someone who will argue with you, challenge your preferred option, and surface the assumptions you did not know you were making.
Most decisions are not made poorly because of missing data. They are made poorly because of unchallenged assumptions. The problem is not analytical capacity but intellectual honesty — and intellectual honesty is hard when everyone in the room has an agenda.
AI has no agenda. Unlike colleagues, it is not protecting turf, managing up, or afraid to disagree with the boss. You can say “argue against my preferred option” and it will do so without hurt feelings, without relationship cost, and without pulling punches. It is a tireless devil’s advocate.
There is a subtler benefit as well. Explaining your reasoning to AI forces you to articulate assumptions you did not know you were making. The act of structuring the problem for the conversation is itself clarifying. Many executives report that the most valuable part of a thinking-partner session is not what AI says but what they realized while formulating the question.

title: 3  AI as Thinking Partner
text:
3.2 Five conversation moves
There are five specific things you can say to AI to sharpen your thinking about any decision.






Figure 3.1: The five thinking-partner moves — each one reveals a different blind spot.



The first is to expand options. Ask: “What options am I not considering?” Most decisions are presented as A-vs-B when there are options C, D, and E that nobody has named.
The second is to challenge. Ask: “Argue against my preferred choice.” This is the classic devil’s-advocate move. Tell AI which option you are leaning toward and ask it to make the strongest case against it.
The third is to surface assumptions. Ask: “What would need to be true for Option B to win?” This reframes the debate from arguing about preferences to identifying testable conditions. It turns a subjective disagreement into an empirical question.
The fourth is the pre-mortem. Ask: “If this decision fails, what is the most likely reason?” Pre-mortems are well-studied in decision science; they reliably surface risks that optimism bias would otherwise hide.
The fifth is to reframe. Ask: “How would a CFO / customer / regulator see this?” Shifting perspective changes what the “real” question is. A question about pricing looks different from the sales team’s perspective than from the finance team’s.

title: 3  AI as Thinking Partner
section: 3.3 Example: should we cut our price?
text:
3.3 Example: should we cut our price?
To see these moves in action, consider a scenario. You are a VP at a mid-size SaaS company. Your product is $99/month. A competitor just launched a similar product at $79. Your CEO wants a recommendation by Friday.
Instead of opening a spreadsheet, you open a conversation.
“We’re thinking about dropping our price from $99 to $79 to match a new competitor. Help me think through whether that’s the right move.”
Claude does not answer yes or no. It reframes the decision. Matching the price is not the only option — what about a new $79 tier with fewer features while keeping the $99 tier? Have you considered adding value at $99 instead of cutting price? A third path: hold the price publicly but offer selective retention discounts to at-risk accounts. Three options instead of two, and the conversation is thirty seconds old.
“I hadn’t considered the tiered approach. What are the risks of just matching their price?”
Claude lays out the risks. A 20% price cut requires 25% more customers just to break even on revenue, and your cost structure does not shrink. It signals to the market that your product was not worth $99. And competitors can cut again — you have entered a race you cannot win on price alone.
“OK, argue the other side. What would need to be true for matching the price to be the right call?”
Claude identifies the conditions. Matching makes sense if price is the primary reason prospects choose the competitor — not features or brand. It also makes sense if your churn data shows price-sensitive customers are already leaving. And if your margins can absorb it: at $79, are you still above breakeven per customer?
“One more thing. If we hold the price and it turns out to be wrong, what’s the most likely reason?”
Claude runs the pre-mortem. The most likely failure mode is that the competitor’s price resets buyer expectations across the market. Six months from now, every sales call opens with “Why are you more expensive?” and close rates drop 15%. The damage is slow and invisible — you do not lose customers in a rush; you just stop winning new ones.
Five minutes of conversation has produced three options instead of two, the risks of each, the conditions that determine which is right, and a failure scenario to monitor. No data was harmed in the making of this decision.

title: 3  AI as Thinking Partner
text:
3.4 Second-order thinking
Most decisions are evaluated on their immediate outcome. Second-order thinking asks: what happens after the first effect?
The prompt is straightforward: “If we do X and it works, what happens next? And after that?”
In the pricing example, the first-order effect of cutting prices is that volume increases. The second-order effect is that competitors respond and margins compress industry-wide. The third-order effect is that customers expect low prices permanently and the brand is repositioned downmarket.
AI makes this easy because it can generate the chain of consequences in seconds. You can also ask for the chain in the opposite direction — “If we do X and it fails, what happens next?” — and compare the two trajectories to find the earliest signal that tells you which path you are on.
The best decision-makers routinely think two moves ahead. AI does not replace that instinct, but it automates the generation of consequences so you can focus on evaluating them.

title: 3  AI as Thinking Partner
text:
3.5 Steelmanning
Steelmanning is the opposite of straw-manning. Instead of constructing a weak version of the opposing argument in order to knock it down, you construct the strongest possible version.
The prompt: “Make the strongest possible case for the option I like least.”
If the steelman is weak, your preferred option is stronger than you thought. If the steelman is compelling, you have discovered a genuine risk. Either way, you learn something. The technique works because AI has no emotional investment in any option — it will argue for the position you dislike just as vigorously as it argues for the one you favor.

title: 3  AI as Thinking Partner
text:
3.6 Scenario planning
Scenario planning structures uncertainty into a small number of concrete futures.
The prompt: “Give me three scenarios — optimistic, base case, pessimistic — with probabilities and specific trigger events.”
The key is specificity. A good scenario is not “things go well” but “the competitor’s product launch underperforms because their enterprise features are six months behind, and we capture 30% of their early adopters by Q3.” Each scenario should name a trigger event that you can monitor. If the trigger fires, you know which scenario you are in and can act accordingly.
Scenario planning is particularly valuable when combined with the pre-mortem. The pessimistic scenario is essentially a pre-mortem with a probability attached.

title: 3  AI as Thinking Partner
text:
3.7 Combining data and thinking
The most powerful conversations blend Module 2 analysis with Module 3 thinking moves.
“Here’s my Module 2 memo. Find the three weakest claims.” This takes the Superstore recommendation you built in the previous module and subjects it to adversarial scrutiny.
“The data says the West region has the highest margin. What alternative explanations fit the same data?” This is the scientific method applied to business analysis: generating alternative hypotheses for the same evidence.
“I recommended expanding product line X. Run a pre-mortem on that recommendation.” This applies the pre-mortem not to a hypothetical scenario but to your own work.

title: 3  AI as Thinking Partner
text:
3.8 Challenging your own analysis
Bring your Module 2 analysis and ask AI to poke holes in it. Open your recommendation memo and ask:

Read this memo. What are the three weakest claims? What data would strengthen or disprove each one?


What did I miss? What alternative explanations fit the same data?

AI catches common blind spots: correlation treated as causation, survivorship bias in the sample, missing context from external events or seasonality. The output is not a new memo but a sharper understanding of the old one. You can then strengthen weak claims with additional data, acknowledge limitations explicitly, or revise the recommendation if warranted.
The memo does not change. Your confidence in it does.

title: 3  AI as Thinking Partner
text:
3.9 From analysis to action
A thinking-partner conversation should end with clarity, not more questions.
Close the conversation with prompts like: “Summarize the three strongest arguments for my recommendation.” “What are the two biggest risks I should name when I present this?” “Write a one-paragraph executive summary that acknowledges the limitations.”
You leave with a recommendation you have stress-tested, risks you can name before someone else does, and language ready for the meeting or memo. The thinking partner does not decide for you. It makes you harder to argue against.

title: 3  AI as Thinking Partner
text:
3.10 Exercises






Exercise 1: The Five-Move Workout





Pick a real decision you are currently facing — work or personal. Have a thinking-partner conversation with Claude in which you use each of the five moves exactly once: expand options, challenge, surface assumptions, pre-mortem, and reframe.
After each move, write one sentence describing what the move revealed. At the end, reflect: did AI change your mind? Did it surface something you had not considered?
Write up the conversation transcript and a five-sentence reflection, one per move.









Exercise 2: Challenge Your Module 2 Analysis





Open the recommendation memo you wrote in Module 2 (or re-run the Superstore analysis using superstore.csv). Give the memo to Claude and ask it to find the three weakest claims.
For each weak claim, ask what data you would need to strengthen it. Then ask Claude to rewrite the executive summary to acknowledge the limitations it identified.
Compare the original and revised summaries. Which one would survive tougher scrutiny in a meeting?









Exercise 3: Challenge Your Retention Memo





Open the retention memo you wrote in Module 2 Exercise 3 (or re-run the attrition analysis using employee-attrition.csv). Give the memo to Claude and work through three thinking-partner moves.
First, ask Claude to argue against your top recommendation. If you recommended reducing overtime, have it make the case that overtime is a symptom, not a cause. If you recommended raising salaries, have it explain why that might not move the needle.
Second, ask: “What would need to be true for my recommendations to fail?” Identify the assumptions your memo depends on.
Third, run a pre-mortem: “We implemented all three interventions and attrition got worse. What happened?”
Revise your memo to acknowledge the strongest counterargument.









Exercise 4: Scenario Swap





If you are working through this course with a colleague, write down a real business decision you are facing in one or two sentences. Swap scenarios. Have a thinking-partner conversation about your colleague’s scenario using at least three of the five conversation moves.
Share back: “Here’s what AI surfaced about your decision.” It is often easier to be objective about someone else’s problem.









Exercise 5: Second-Order Thinking Practice





Consider the following decision: “We are going to require all employees to return to the office five days a week.”
Ask Claude to trace three levels of consequences if this decision works as intended. Then ask it to trace three levels of consequences if it does not work as intended.
Compare the two chains. Where do they diverge? What is the earliest signal that tells you which path you are on?









Exercise 6: Stakeholder Simulation





Write a one-paragraph proposal — a new initiative, a policy change, or a budget request.
Ask Claude to react to the proposal as four different stakeholders: your CEO, your CFO, a skeptical board member, and a front-line employee. For each persona, note the main concern and what would win them over.
Revise the proposal to address the top two concerns.

title: 4  Connecting Databases
text:
4.1 From spreadsheets to databases
Module 1 described the vision: replacing dashboards with natural-language queries. This module makes it real. You will connect a coding agent to a database and build the tools that let anyone query it in plain English — no SQL required.
The first two modules worked with individual files — a CSV placed in a folder, a memo pasted into a conversation. Most real business data does not live in spreadsheets. It lives in databases: structured collections of related tables, hosted on servers, queried with SQL.
This module connects AI to those databases. By the end, you will understand how your organization can create tools so that everyone can query a database in plain English — no SQL required. As throughout this course, the examples use Claude Code, but the same workflows apply to other coding agents (see Appendix).
In Module 2 you analyzed a spreadsheet — a single table of rows and columns living in a file on your machine. Most real business data lives in databases: many related tables linked by keys, hosted on a server, queried with SQL.
SQL is the language databases understand. You do not need to learn it. AI writes the SQL for you. But you do need to tell the AI what is in the database — which tables exist, what the columns mean, and how the tables relate to each other.

title: 4  Connecting Databases
section: 4.2 How AI connects to a database
text:
4.2 How AI connects to a database
There are several ways to give AI access to your data, ranging from simple to sophisticated.
Skill files. The simplest approach: write a markdown file that describes your database schema and hand it to a coding agent. The agent reads the description, writes SQL, and executes it. No special infrastructure is needed — just a file and a coding agent that can run code. This is what we build in this module.
Direct API calls. A lightweight application — even fifty lines of Python — can send a user’s question and a schema description to an LLM, get back SQL, run it, and display the results. This is the pattern behind the demo app at the end of this module. It works for prototypes and internal tools where you control the deployment.
MCP (Model Context Protocol). An open standard that lets AI connect to external tools — databases, CRMs, calendars, anything with an API. Think of it as USB for AI. The AI assistant acts as an MCP client and connects to MCP servers that wrap external systems. MCP is most useful when you need to connect to many different tools in a single conversation or when IT wants a standardized way to manage AI’s access to corporate systems.
Vendor platforms. Salesforce, Snowflake, Databricks, and others are embedding natural-language query directly into their products. If your data already lives in one of these platforms, you may not need to build anything — just enable the feature.
All of these approaches share the same core idea: give the AI a description of your data and let it write the query. The differences are in how the description is delivered, who runs the query, and how much infrastructure is involved. We start with skill files because they require no setup and teach the underlying pattern that every other approach builds on.






flowchart LR
    A["📄 Skill File\n_Markdown + agent_\nNo setup needed"]
    B["🐍 Direct API\n_Python app + LLM_\nLight infrastructure"]
    C["🔌 MCP\n_Standardized protocol_\nMulti-tool integration"]
    D["🏢 Vendor Platform\n_Snowflake, Salesforce_\nBuilt-in AI features"]
    A -->|"more infrastructure →"| B -->|"more infrastructure →"| C -->|"more infrastructure →"| D

    style A fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#4caf50
    style B fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#2196f3

Figure 4.1: Four ways to connect AI to your data — from simplest to most integrated.

title: 4  Connecting Databases
text:
4.3 The sample database: Chinook
The exercises in this module use the Chinook sample database, which you can download here: chinook.db. It represents a music retailer’s operations and contains 11 related tables.
The key tables are employees (8 staff members), customers (59 customers across 24 countries), invoices and invoice_items (412 transactions), and tracks, albums, artists, and genres (a product catalog of more than 3,500 tracks). It is a publicly available SQLite file that requires no setup — just place it in a folder and point your coding agent at it. The same skill pattern you learn here works with any database.

title: 4  Connecting Databases
text:
4.4 What is a skill?
Out of the box, the coding agent can read files, write code, and run it. But you can extend what it knows how to do by installing skills — packaged instructions that teach the agent a specific workflow.
Claude Code has a skill system built in. A skill is a folder containing a markdown file that describes the task, the steps, and the output format. When you invoke a skill, Claude Code reads the instructions and follows them. The installer from the preface already set up four skills that are particularly useful for business work:

xlsx — read, create, and edit Excel spreadsheets with formulas, formatting, and charts.
docx — create and edit Word documents with tracked changes, comments, and formatting.
pptx — create and edit PowerPoint presentations.
skill-creator — a guide for building your own skills. You will use this later in this module when you create a database skill.

You can verify the skills are installed by opening the Code tab and typing / — the available skills appear as slash commands.
A skill is a set of instructions that specializes a general-purpose AI for a specific task. Every AI customization works the same way: additional text is added to the prompt. A skill provides three things: domain knowledge (what the data means), workflow instructions (how to do the task), and output format specifications (how to present results).
Skills are not limited to databases. You could build a skill for weekly report generation, customer support analysis, or any recurring task where AI benefits from domain context.

title: 4  Connecting Databases
text:
4.5 The SKILL.md file
The skill is a markdown file that the coding agent reads when you invoke it. The structure has three parts.
The first part is the description and location: what the skill does and the path to the database file.
The second part is the schema and relationships: every table, its columns, data types, and how tables link to each other. This is the most important part. The more precisely you describe the schema, the better the AI’s queries will be. Include column descriptions (“status is one of: active, completed, on-hold”), explain join paths (“invoices link to customers via CustomerId”), and note any business logic (“revenue means invoice_items.UnitPrice times Quantity”).
The third part is the instructions: query style, output format, and chart preferences. For example: “Always show the SQL query before showing results. Use Plotly for interactive charts. Annotate the highest and lowest values.”
The skill file is just text. Anyone can edit it. No programming is required — just clear descriptions of your data.

title: 4  Connecting Databases
section: 4.6 Creating the skill step by step
text:
4.6 Creating the skill step by step
Create a project folder with the SQLite database file and a file called SKILL.md. Write the schema, describing each table, its columns, and its relationships. Then test by asking questions and refining based on what Claude gets wrong.
Your first draft will not be perfect, and that is fine. The skill improves through iteration. Every time Claude misinterprets a question or uses the wrong join path, you add a clarification to the SKILL.md. After a few rounds of testing, the skill handles the most common questions reliably.
You do not have to write the skill from scratch. Point your coding agent at the database and ask it to generate the SKILL.md for you. It will inspect the tables, columns, and foreign keys and produce a first draft. Your job is to read the result, verify that the descriptions match your understanding of the data, and add any business context the agent could not infer on its own.

title: 4  Connecting Databases
text:
4.7 Building the Chinook skill
To make this concrete, consider a scenario. You are an operations director at a music retailer. The CEO wants a recommendation by Friday: which markets to invest in, which product lines to promote, and how to improve per-customer revenue.
The data lives in the Chinook database. The answer requires joining customer, invoice, and catalog data across multiple tables. There is no time to wait for an analyst.
The solution: build a skill that knows the Chinook schema, then ask questions in plain English. The agent writes SQL, runs it, and returns charts.

title: 4  Connecting Databases
text:
4.8 Decision-driven queries
With the skill active, every question should connect to the CEO’s decision.
“Top five countries by revenue, bar chart, sorted” produces a bar chart comparing categories. “Monthly revenue trend line — is the business growing?” produces a line chart showing trends over time. “Plot average order value versus number of orders by country” produces a scatter plot that reveals clusters of high-value versus high-frequency markets.
You can go deeper. “Correlation heatmap of all numeric columns” shows which metrics move together. “Make that chart interactive so I can hover for details” switches from matplotlib to Plotly, saving the result as an HTML file. “Annotate the outlier country on the scatter plot” adds context to the visualization.
Each question is a different lens on the same decision. The skill ensures that every query uses the correct tables, joins, and business logic.

title: 4  Connecting Databases
text:
4.9 SQL transparency
One of the most important features of this workflow is that you can always see the SQL. When you ask “Which country generates the most revenue? Show a bar chart of the top 10,” Claude generates a SQL query that selects country and summed revenue from the invoices and customers tables, groups by country, orders by revenue descending, and limits to ten results.
You can read the SQL and verify that the joins are correct, the filters make sense, and the aggregation matches your intent. Analysts can review the logic. This builds trust: AI is not a black box.
If you ask a follow-up — “Break down the US revenue by state” — Claude writes a new query with a WHERE clause and additional grouping. The SQL is always available for inspection.

title: 4  Connecting Databases
text:
4.10 Iterating on the skill
Common refinements include adding column descriptions, specifying default sorting and format rules, and setting chart preferences. You can specify whether charts should be static (matplotlib, good for reports and slides) or interactive (Plotly, with hover, zoom, and filter, saved as HTML files and shareable via email). The same prompt works for both — just add “interactive” or “Plotly.”
Skills turn institutional knowledge into executable instructions. Instead of a procedures manual that sits on a shelf, you have a prompt that AI follows every time. The knowledge is not just documented; it is operationalized.

title: 4  Connecting Databases
text:
4.11 Behind the firewall
Understanding the data flow matters for security. When you use a coding agent, the flow works like this: you type a question, the agent sends your question and the schema description to the LLM (hosted in the cloud), the LLM returns SQL, and the agent executes that SQL against your database locally. The raw data never leaves your network — only your question and the schema travel to the cloud. The query results stay on your machine.
In a deployed app (like the Streamlit demo), the app replaces the coding agent: the app sends the question to the LLM, receives SQL back, runs it against the database, and displays results to the user. Again, the LLM never sees the actual data — only the question and the schema.
IT controls which databases the agent or app can access and can restrict connections to read-only credentials.
For organizations that need to connect AI to multiple systems — databases, CRMs, financial data providers, project management tools — MCP provides a standardized way to manage those connections. The next section explains how it works.
In this module we connect to a local SQLite file. In your organization, the same pattern connects to PostgreSQL, Snowflake, or any database your team uses.

title: 4  Connecting Databases
text:
4.12 MCP: connecting to everything else
The skill-file approach works well when AI needs access to one database. But organizations often need AI to reach many systems at once — databases, CRMs, financial data providers, project management tools. This is what MCP (Model Context Protocol) is for.
MCP is an open standard — think of it as USB for AI. A coding agent acts as an MCP client. Each external system is wrapped in an MCP server — a small program that advertises the tools it offers (query a database, look up a customer, fetch a stock price). When you connect an MCP server, its tools appear automatically in the agent’s toolkit. The agent decides which tools to call based on your question. You can connect multiple servers at once, and the agent sees all of their tools in a single conversation.






flowchart LR

    DB["🗄️ Database Server\n_Snowflake, PostgreSQL_"]
    FIN["📊 Financial Data\n_FactSet, Bloomberg_"]
    CRM["👥 CRM Server\n_Salesforce, HubSpot_"]
    COLLAB["💬 Collaboration\n_Slack, GitHub, Drive_"]

    Client <-- "MCP" --> DB
    Client <-- "MCP" --> FIN
    Client <-- "MCP" --> CRM
    Client <-- "MCP" --> COLLAB

    style Client fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#2196f3
    style DB fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#4caf50
    style FIN fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#ff9800
    style CRM fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#9c27b0
    style COLLAB fill:#fce4ec,stroke:#e91e63




Figure 4.2: MCP connects one AI client to many servers — each wrapping a different external system.





Each MCP server handles authentication, rate limiting, and data formatting so the agent does not have to. The agent never sees API keys or raw API responses — the server manages those details and returns clean, structured results.
In Claude Desktop, MCP servers are configured in a JSON settings file or installed as one-click desktop extensions. Once configured, a server’s tools are available every time you open the Code tab.

Corporate MCP servers
Major platforms and data providers now offer MCP servers. The list is growing quickly. Here is a sample of what is available:
Databases and data platforms — Snowflake, Databricks (Unity Catalog, Vector Search, Genie), Google BigQuery, PostgreSQL, MongoDB.
Financial data — FactSet (fundamentals, M&A, supply chain), Bloomberg (internal adoption), LSEG/Refinitiv, Moody’s, S&P Global, Nasdaq, Alpha Vantage (stock prices, options, forex, economic indicators), Financial Modeling Prep (financial statements, SEC filings).
CRM and ERP — Salesforce (Agentforce), HubSpot, Microsoft Dynamics 365, SAP.
Business intelligence — Power BI (query semantic models, create measures and relationships).
Cloud platforms — AWS (60+ servers spanning infrastructure, Lambda, analytics), Azure (40+ servers), Google Cloud.
Collaboration — GitHub, Slack, Notion, Google Drive, Asana, Jira, Linear.
Payments — Stripe, PayPal.
Universal connectors — CData Connect AI (350+ enterprise data sources through a single MCP interface), Workato (100+ pre-built servers).
The MCP registry at registry.modelcontextprotocol.io lists thousands of public servers, and enterprises can run private registries for internal tools.

title: 4  Connecting Databases
text:
4.13 The skill as deployment artifact
A polished skill is what gets deployed on your intranet. The skill file encodes which tables matter, how they relate, and what the business terminology means. IT adds the infrastructure: SSO authentication, read-only database credentials, and query logging for audit. Module 6 shows how to chain this skill into an automated reporting pipeline.

title: 4  Connecting Databases
text:
4.14 A natural-language query app
The skill-file approach works well with a coding agent, but what about the rest of your organization? Not everyone will use a terminal. The same pattern — send a question and schema to an LLM, get SQL back, run it — can be wrapped in a simple web app that anyone can use from a browser.
In production, this app would be deployed on the corporate intranet, the database would be a PostgreSQL or Snowflake instance on the company’s servers, and the LLM would be an enterprise-licensed model (more on licensing and compliance in Module 5). For this demo, we run everything locally: a small Streamlit app on your laptop, the Chinook SQLite file in the same folder, and Claude Sonnet via the Anthropic API.

Setting up the Anthropic API
The demo apps call Claude Sonnet through the Anthropic API. You will need an API key.

Sign up at console.anthropic.com and add a payment method.
Create an API key under Settings → API Keys.
Set the key as an environment variable so the demo apps can find it:
Mac / Linux — add to your shell profile (.bashrc or .zshrc) to persist:
export ANTHROPIC_API_KEY="sk-ant-..."
Windows — run once in a terminal, then restart the terminal:
setx ANTHROPIC_API_KEY "sk-ant-..."

API calls are billed per token. A short request costs a fraction of a cent; a longer conversation might cost a few cents to a few dollars. Current prices are listed at anthropic.com/pricing.
Download the app files here: chinook-query.py and requirements.txt. Copy chinook.db into the same folder, install the dependencies by running pip install -r requirements.txt in a terminal or by asking your coding agent to install them, and then run:
streamlit run chinook-query.py


What the app sends to the LLM
When you type a question, the app sends two things to the LLM: a system prompt containing the database schema, and your question. Here is the system prompt (abbreviated):
You are a SQL assistant. Given the schema below and a user
question, return ONLY a single SQLite SELECT statement.

The Chinook database has these tables:

artists(ArtistId, Name)
albums(AlbumId, Title, ArtistId) → artists
tracks(TrackId, Name, AlbumId, GenreId, UnitPrice) → albums, genres
invoices(InvoiceId, CustomerId, InvoiceDate, Total) → customers
invoice_items(InvoiceLineId, InvoiceId, TrackId, UnitPrice, Quantity)
...

Revenue = SUM(invoice_items.UnitPrice * invoice_items.Quantity).
Notice what is not in this prompt: no actual data. The LLM sees only the table and column names — the same schema description you would put in a skill file.


What comes back
If you ask “What are the top 5 countries by revenue?”, the LLM returns pure SQL:
SELECT c.Country, SUM(ii.UnitPrice * ii.Quantity) AS Revenue
FROM invoice_items ii
JOIN invoices i ON ii.InvoiceId = i.InvoiceId
JOIN customers c ON i.CustomerId = c.CustomerId
GROUP BY c.Country
ORDER BY Revenue DESC
LIMIT 5;
The app runs this query against the local chinook.db file and displays the results in a table. The generated SQL is shown above the results — the same transparency principle discussed in the “SQL transparency” section. The LLM never sees the query results; it only writes the SQL.


The data flow
The complete round trip looks like this:

You type a question in the browser.
The app sends your question + the schema description to the LLM (cloud).
The LLM returns a SQL query (cloud → your machine).
The app executes the SQL against the database (entirely local).
The app displays the results in your browser (entirely local).

The only things that leave your machine are the question and the schema. The actual data — customer names, revenue figures, transaction records — never goes to the cloud.
