title: 4  Connecting Databases
text:
4.15 Adding charts and visualizations
The query app returns tables, but business questions often need charts. “Show me a bar chart of revenue by country” should produce a bar chart, not a grid of numbers. With a coding agent like Claude Code, you would get the chart directly — the agent writes Python, executes it, and displays the image. But a deployed web app cannot do that unless we change what the LLM returns.
The fix is straightforward: instead of asking the LLM for SQL, ask it for a short Python script that queries the database and produces a chart. The app executes that Python and displays whatever it creates — a matplotlib figure, a Plotly chart, a data table, or all three.

The changed system prompt
The original query app’s system prompt said: return ONLY a single SQLite SELECT statement. The chart app replaces that with:
You are a Python code assistant for the Chinook music database.
Given the schema below and a user question, return ONLY a
Python script.

Rules for the Python script:
- Use sqlite3 to query the database. The connection is
  already available as `conn`.
- Use pandas for data manipulation.
- If the question asks for a chart, use matplotlib.
  Call st.pyplot(fig) to display it.
- Always store query results in a DataFrame called `df`.
The schema description is identical — the LLM still sees only table and column names, never the actual data.


What comes back
If you ask “Bar chart of the top 5 countries by revenue,” the LLM now returns Python instead of SQL:
df = pd.read_sql_query("""
    SELECT c.Country, SUM(ii.UnitPrice * ii.Quantity) AS Revenue
    FROM invoice_items ii
    JOIN invoices i ON ii.InvoiceId = i.InvoiceId
    JOIN customers c ON i.CustomerId = c.CustomerId
    GROUP BY c.Country
    ORDER BY Revenue DESC
    LIMIT 5
""", conn)

fig, ax = plt.subplots()
ax.bar(df["Country"], df["Revenue"])
ax.set_ylabel("Revenue ($)")
ax.set_title("Top 5 Countries by Revenue")
st.pyplot(fig)
The SQL is still there — embedded inside a read_sql_query call — but now the script also creates the chart. The app executes this code and displays both the chart and the underlying data table.


The data flow is unchanged
The security model is the same as the SQL-only app. The LLM sees only the question and the schema. The Python executes locally against the local database. No data leaves your machine. The difference is that the app now executes Python rather than a single SQL statement, which gives the LLM more expressive power — it can sort, reshape, and visualize the data in ways that raw SQL cannot.
Download the chart app files here: chinook-charts.py and requirements.txt. Copy chinook.db into the same folder, install the dependencies by running pip install -r requirements.txt in a terminal or by asking your coding agent to install them, and then run:
streamlit run chinook-charts.py


A note on executing generated code
The chart app uses Python’s exec() to run LLM-generated code. For a local demo this is fine — you are running it on your own machine. In a production deployment, executing AI-generated code requires sandboxing: running the code in an isolated container with no network access and limited filesystem permissions. Module 6 discusses sandboxing in more detail, and Module 5 covers the broader governance framework.


From demo to production
This is a demo, not a production application. There is no authentication, no guardrails on the SQL, and no sandboxing of query execution. The chart app adds another consideration: it executes LLM-generated Python, not just SQL, which makes sandboxing even more important. In a production deployment, IT would add SSO login, restrict the database connection to read-only credentials, log every query for audit, run generated code in an isolated container, and use an enterprise LLM with a data-processing agreement. Module 5 covers these considerations in detail. But as a prototype that proves the concept, fifty lines of Python is all it takes.

title: 4  Connecting Databases
text:
4.16 Exercises






Exercise 1: Build a Chinook Skill





Download chinook.db and place it in a new project folder. Create a SKILL.md file in the same folder with three sections: a description and database path, at least three table descriptions with columns and relationships, and three example questions.
Open your coding agent in the folder and test three decision-relevant queries. For each, ask Claude to show the SQL and verify that the joins and filters are correct. Then ask for an interactive Plotly chart and iterate on it at least once — change colors, add annotations, or improve the layout.
You should end up with a working SKILL.md and at least one interactive chart.









Exercise 2: Database Schema Detective





Connect your coding agent to the Chinook database without any SKILL.md — no documentation at all.
Ask: “What tables are in this database? How are they related?” Let Claude discover the schema on its own. Then ask: “If I were the CEO of this music company, what three questions should I be asking?”
Follow up with two questions that require joining at least two tables (for example, “Revenue by artist” requires joining invoices, invoice_items, tracks, albums, and artists).
Compare the agent’s schema discovery to what you know from the module. Did it miss anything?









Exercise 3: Decision Scenario Queries





You are the Chinook CEO. The board wants you to cut two of the five lowest-performing genres from the catalog to reduce licensing costs.
Using your Chinook skill (or a bare connection to chinook.db), answer these questions in sequence: Which genres contribute the least revenue? For those two genres, how many unique customers would be affected? Are any of those customers high-value customers who also buy other genres?
Write one sentence stating which two genres you would cut and why.









Exercise 4: Skill for Your Own Data





Take a CSV from your work (anonymize if needed) or a public dataset you care about. Ask the agent to convert it to a SQLite database. Then write a SKILL.md describing the tables, columns, relationships, and five example questions.
If you do not have a work dataset, try one of these public sources:

Kaggle Datasets — thousands of downloadable CSVs across business, finance, healthcare, sports, and more.
UCI Machine Learning Repository — classic datasets for analysis, including census, retail, and banking data.
data.gov — U.S. government open data on economics, education, health, and public safety.
World Bank Open Data — country-level economic and development indicators.

Test five queries and refine the skill until all five return correct results.









Exercise 5: The Monday Morning Report





Identify a recurring report your team produces — weekly sales summary, monthly KPIs, pipeline report. Write a SKILL.md that includes the database schema, the specific queries needed, and the output format.
Add an instruction to the skill: “When I say ‘Monday report,’ run these three queries and format the results as a one-page summary.” Test it. Does one command produce your entire report?









Exercise 6: Build Your Own Query App





Ask your coding agent to build a natural-language query app similar to the chinook-query demo, either from scratch or by improving the demo. Possible extensions: add chart generation for numeric results, show query history in a sidebar, add error handling that suggests corrections when the SQL fails, or let the user choose between different databases.

title: 5  Reliability and Compliance
text:
5.1 The trust spectrum
The previous four modules built an increasingly powerful toolkit: agent concepts, data analysis, thinking-partner conversations, and database skills. The next three modules will add agent workflows and document-based AI. Before any of this is deployed, leadership will ask one question: can we trust it?
This module provides the answer, which is yes — with the right verification. You will learn a repeatable protocol for validating AI outputs, stress-testing skills and RAG systems, and building the governance framework your organization needs.
AI with code execution is like a fast junior analyst — capable but in need of oversight. The appropriate level of oversight depends on the stakes.
For low-stakes work (exploratory charts, directional trends, internal brainstorming), a quick sanity check is sufficient. Glance at the numbers, confirm they are in the right ballpark, and move on.
For medium-stakes work (team skills from Module 4, recurring reports, RAG-based Q&A from Module 8), test with known answers before relying on the results. Run queries where you already know the answer and confirm that AI matches.
For high-stakes work (board presentations, budget recommendations, external reporting), apply the full verification protocol described later in this module. Every number, every chart, every claim should be independently verified.

title: 5  Reliability and Compliance
text:
Key Terms






Hallucination — AI generating plausible but incorrect output (wrong numbers, fabricated citations, confident but false claims).
RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation) — grounding LLM responses in retrieved documents so answers cite real sources rather than relying on the model’s training data alone.
Red-teaming — deliberately attacking your own system to find weaknesses before real users or adversaries do.1
Guardrails — technical constraints that prevent misuse: read-only database access, restricted tables, input validation, and query logging.
Defense in depth — layering multiple independent safeguards so that no single failure compromises the system.
PII (Personally Identifiable Information) — data that can identify an individual (names, Social Security numbers, email addresses, etc.).
Data sovereignty — the legal requirement that data remain under a specific jurisdiction’s control.2
Air-gapped — infrastructure with no internet connectivity, used for classified or highly sensitive workloads.

title: 5  Reliability and Compliance
text:
5.2 Hallucination: the core risk
“How do I know the AI isn’t making things up?”
AI in code-execution mode does not invent facts the way a chatbot might. It reads real data and runs real computations. But it makes silent analytical errors: wrong filters, dropped rows, incorrect joins, misinterpreted column names. In RAG mode, it can also hallucinate citations — citing a page that does not actually contain the claim.
These errors are dangerous precisely because the output looks professional. Charts render cleanly even when the underlying data is wrong. Memos read smoothly even when the conclusion does not follow from the evidence. A polished result inspires confidence, and confidence without verification is the real risk.
You have already seen examples. Date or currency parsing surprises in Module 2. Incorrect join paths in Module 4. Module 8 will show how RAG can produce hallucinated citations. Each error is fixable, but only if someone checks.

title: 5  Reliability and Compliance
text:
5.3 The verification protocol
A three-step process you can apply to any AI-generated analysis.
The first step is to sanity-check with known answers. Ask questions where you already know the result: row count, column names, a total you can verify in Excel. “How many rows are in this dataset?” “What is the total revenue?” If AI’s answer does not match your independently verified number, stop and investigate before proceeding.
The second step is to inspect the methodology. Ask “show me the code” or “show me the SQL.” Read the logic, not just the output. Check that the joins are correct, the filters make sense, and the aggregation matches your intent. Then rephrase the question and run it again. If the answers agree, you have confirmation. If they differ, one approach is wrong.
The third step is to spot-check edge cases. Test the smallest group, zero values, null fields, and boundary dates. Errors hide at the edges. A query that works perfectly for the top ten countries may break for the bottom three.
If you would spot-check a human analyst’s work, spot-check AI’s.

title: 5  Reliability and Compliance
text:
5.4 Catching silent errors
To make this concrete, consider a deliberately tricky prompt.
“What’s the average profit margin by sub-category? Exclude any sub-categories with negative total profit.”
The trap is in the filter logic. Does AI filter before or after aggregating? Does it drop individual rows with negative profit, or sub-categories whose total profit is negative? The order matters, and AI often gets it wrong.
To catch it, ask three follow-up questions. “Show me the code” — inspect the filter logic. “How many rows are in the result?” — does the count match your expectation? “List the excluded sub-categories” — are they the right ones?
The more specific the filter logic, the more likely AI misinterprets it.

title: 5  Reliability and Compliance
text:
5.5 Trusting RAG answers
RAG (Module 8) grounds AI in your documents, but grounding does not guarantee correctness.
RAG introduces three specific risks. The first is hallucinated citations: the LLM cites a page or section that does not actually contain the claim. The citation looks authoritative, but when you check the source, the passage says something different or does not address the question at all.
The second is stale documents. If a policy changed but the old version is still in the index, RAG will retrieve and cite the outdated version. The answer is grounded — just in the wrong version of the truth.
The third is wrong-chunk retrieval. The retrieved passage is semantically similar to the question but does not actually answer it. This happens when the document contains multiple sections that use similar language for different purposes.
The verification approach is the same: click through every citation and confirm that the cited passage actually says what the LLM claims. Check document dates to ensure you are getting the current version. Ask the same question in different words to see if the citation changes.
A citation that looks right but is not is worse than no citation at all. It creates false confidence.

title: 5  Reliability and Compliance
text:
5.6 Red-teaming RAG
Red-teaming means deliberately attacking your own system to find weaknesses before real users do.3 Test your RAG system the way an adversary would.
Ask out-of-scope questions: “What is our competitor’s revenue?” when no competitor data is in the documents. Does AI admit it does not know, or does it hallucinate an answer?
Upload two documents that disagree on a fact and ask about the disputed point. Does AI cite both? Pick one? Acknowledge the conflict?
Ask boundary questions about topics at the edge of what is documented. These are the questions most likely to produce hallucinated citations, because the system retrieves a passage that is close but not quite on point.
Every failure you find now is one your team will not encounter in production.

title: 5  Reliability and Compliance
text:
5.7 Red-teaming your skill
The database skill from Module 4 is the kind of tool people stop questioning once it is deployed. Test it like any tool your team depends on.4
Validate with known answers by running queries where you already know the result (total invoices, number of customers). Compare AI’s SQL to a hand-written query. Ask the same question multiple times and check for consistency.
Stress-test with edge cases. Ask for revenue from a nonexistent country. Query boundary dates (the first and last month in the dataset). Use ambiguous column names (“show me the name” when multiple tables have a name column).
For each failure, diagnose the root cause (bad SQL, missing context, or wrong table), fix the SKILL.md, and retest. This is how production skills get hardened. Every failure makes the skill better.

title: 5  Reliability and Compliance
text:
5.8 Three layers of auditability
Production AI systems should have multiple layers of verification.
The first layer is RAG: the LLM answers based on your documents, with citations. This grounds responses in source material. You verify by checking citations against the original text.
The second layer is code transparency: the agent displays the SQL or Python it ran. Analysts review the logic, not just the answer. This is what you practiced in Modules 2 through 4.
The third layer is guardrails: read-only database access, restricted tables, and every query logged for audit. The system prevents mistakes, not just detects them.
The more layers you add, the harder it is for errors to reach your team. Defense in depth — the same principle as cybersecurity.

title: 5  Reliability and Compliance
text:
5.9 Security and governance
Before deploying AI with your data, three questions need clear answers: what data leaves your network, where does inference happen, and what audit trail exists?

Cloud API
When you call a cloud API — OpenAI, Anthropic, Google — your application sends a prompt to the provider’s shared infrastructure, the LLM runs inference there, and the response comes back. The key question is what is in that prompt.
As Module 4 showed, you often need to send only a schema description and a user question. The LLM returns SQL or code, and your application executes it locally against your own database. The actual data — customer names, revenue figures, health records — never leaves your network. In this pattern, cloud inference is compatible with most data policies because the sensitive data stays behind your firewall.
However, some use cases do require sending data to the LLM. RAG prompts include document chunks. Summarization tasks send the full text to be summarized. In these cases, the data does travel to the provider’s servers, and the provider’s data-processing agreement governs what happens to it.
Enterprise API tiers from Anthropic, OpenAI, and Google typically guarantee that your data is not used for training, offer short or zero retention windows, and provide contractual commitments on data handling. Consumer tiers (free ChatGPT, free Claude) may not offer these guarantees. Always confirm the terms before sending sensitive data.


Dedicated cloud tenancy
Dedicated cloud tenancy means running inference on isolated instances within a public cloud provider — hardware in the provider’s data center but reserved exclusively for your organization and walled off from other tenants.5 Examples include AWS Dedicated Hosts, Azure Dedicated Host, and GCP sole-tenant nodes.6
For closed-source models, the providers offer dedicated deployment options. Anthropic offers Claude on Amazon Bedrock and Google Cloud Vertex AI, where inference runs in your dedicated tenancy under your security controls. OpenAI offers Azure OpenAI Service, where GPT models run in your Azure subscription. These are enterprise licensing arrangements: you pay for compute and per-token usage, and the provider guarantees that your data stays within your tenancy and is not used for training.
Dedicated tenancy gives you the security isolation of on-premise infrastructure with the operational convenience of a cloud provider. It is a common choice for organizations that need strong data controls but do not want to manage their own GPU hardware.


Private cloud and on-premise inference
Private cloud means running the LLM on servers your organization owns or operates — in your own data center, with cloud-like characteristics such as elastic scaling and self-service provisioning.7 Examples include HPE GreenLake and Dell APEX. On-premise deployments without these cloud characteristics are simply traditional data-center hosting. In either case, nothing leaves your network. You control the hardware, the model weights, and the logs.
For open-source models (Llama, Mistral, DeepSeek), you download the model weights and run inference on your own GPU servers. There is no vendor relationship for the model itself — you manage everything.
On-premise and private-cloud deployments give maximum control but require GPU infrastructure and operational expertise. They may also lag behind the latest cloud-hosted models in capability.
Air-gapped environments — on-premise infrastructure with no internet connectivity — represent the most restrictive deployment. These are required for classified and military workloads where even outbound network traffic is prohibited.


Choosing the right deployment
Most organizations need a mix. Cloud APIs work well for non-sensitive tasks where only schema or public information is sent. Dedicated cloud tenancy suits workloads that need isolation without the burden of managing hardware. Private cloud or on-premise deployment is appropriate for the most sensitive workloads: regulated records, proprietary documents, or data subject to strict sovereignty requirements.
The deciding factor is not the model but the data. Ask: does this prompt contain information that cannot leave our network? If yes, run inference in a dedicated tenancy or on-premise. If no, a cloud API with an enterprise data-processing agreement is typically sufficient.


Data sovereignty
Data sovereignty is the legal and organizational requirement that data remain under the jurisdiction and governance controls of a specific country or entity.8 It goes beyond the cloud-vs.-on-prem deployment choice: even data stored in a cloud provider’s data center may violate sovereignty requirements if that data center is in another country or subject to another government’s laws.
GDPR requires that EU residents’ personal data be processed under EU-adequate protections. U.S. defense agencies require air-gapped environments — infrastructure with no internet connectivity — for classified workloads. Financial regulators in many countries require that customer records stay within national borders. When you connect an AI agent to enterprise data, these constraints determine not just how inference runs but where it runs.
The pattern emerging across industries is hybrid: sensitive and regulated workloads run on private infrastructure (on-premises or sovereign cloud), while non-sensitive tasks use public cloud APIs. The U.S. Department of Defense awarded a $931 million contract to modernize its data centers with on-premise private cloud. Barclays migrated over 50,000 workloads to private cloud infrastructure for regulatory compliance. New Zealand telecom provider 2degrees built a sovereign AI platform to ensure customer data stays under New Zealand jurisdiction. These are not edge cases — they reflect the standard enterprise pattern for AI deployment with sensitive data.


Audit requirements
SOX, GDPR, HIPAA, and industry-specific regulations all impose requirements on how data is processed and by whom.9 Every prompt sent to an LLM is a data-processing event and must be treated accordingly — logged, governed, and reviewed.

title: 5  Reliability and Compliance
text:
5.10 Data policies: training and retention
Before sending data to any AI provider, know the answers to two questions.
Will they train on your data? Consumer tiers (free ChatGPT, free Claude) may use your input to improve their models. API and enterprise tiers typically do not. Check your agreement and confirm that any opt-out is active.
How long do they retain it? Providers retain prompts for a window (often 30 days) for safety monitoring. Enterprise contracts may offer zero retention. Know the policy before you send sensitive data.
Ask your vendor three things: Do you train on our data? How long do you retain it? Can we request deletion?

title: 5  Reliability and Compliance
text:
5.11 Compliance in practice
FERPA protects student education records. HIPAA protects health information. SOX governs financial reporting. GDPR regulates personal data in the EU. Every regulated industry has its version of the same concern: where does the data go, and who can see it?
AI can analyze sensitive data without ever reading the actual records. The technique from Module 4 applies: describe the table structure in the SKILL.md (table names, column names, data types), and the agent writes SQL from the schema alone, never seeing the actual records. The script runs locally, and the output stays on your server.
Table metadata is not PII. The actual records stay behind the firewall. The LLM produces the code, not the output. This pattern — send the schema, keep the data — works for any regulated environment.
You can improve the quality of the generated SQL by including a few synthetic rows alongside the schema. Databricks found that adding approximately three sample rows per table raises SQL accuracy by about six percentage points on standard benchmarks compared to schema alone. The sample rows reveal data formatting patterns (is the status column stored as Active/Inactive or 1/0?), disambiguate column semantics (is code a country code or a product code?), and help the model write correct WHERE clauses with properly formatted literal values. The key is that the rows are synthetic — fabricated to match the real data’s formats and value ranges without containing any actual records. Three rows is the sweet spot; more than that tends to distract the model rather than help it. Keep the synthetic data in sync with your schema: when you add a column or change a data type in production, update the synthetic rows to match.

title: 5  Reliability and Compliance
text:
5.12 Session log auditing
Every AI conversation can be exported as a transcript. The transcript contains the user’s questions, the AI’s responses, the code it generated, and the results it produced. This is the paper trail your compliance team needs.
Review session logs for three things: PII or proprietary data that should stay internal, SQL or Python logic errors in the generated code, and RAG citations that do not match the source.
Export logs, archive them, and review them periodically. Session logs are not just a compliance requirement; they are a learning tool. Reviewing what went wrong in past sessions tells you exactly what to improve in your SKILL.md or RAG configuration.

title: 5  Reliability and Compliance
text:
5.13 The sandbox-audit-deploy pattern
Before deploying any AI workflow to your team, follow three phases.
In the sandbox phase, run the full workflow with synthetic or test data. Test edge cases. Break it on purpose. This is where you discover the failure modes described throughout this module.
In the audit phase, export session logs and review the generated code. Verify SQL logic. Check RAG citations. Look for data leakage. Have a colleague who did not build the system try to break it.
In the deploy phase, roll out to users with monitoring. Review logs weekly. Set up alerting for anomalies. Treat the first month as a soft launch.

title: 5  Reliability and Compliance
text:
5.14 The adoption roadmap
Each stage of AI adoption maps to a trust level.
Quick wins, achievable in weeks, require only light verification. Using AI for research, summarization, and email drafting. Analyzing local data with a coding agent (Module 2). Having thinking-partner conversations (Module 3).
Medium-term adoption, taking months, requires test-before-deploy discipline. Skills for recurring database workflows (Module 4). Agent-powered reports and pipelines (Module 6). RAG for document-based Q&A (Module 8).
Strategic adoption, measured in quarters, requires a full audit trail. Production apps with authentication and audit trails. An AI usage policy, compliance review, and governance framework. Organization-wide adoption with monitoring.

title: 5  Reliability and Compliance
text:
5.15 Exercises






Exercise 1: The Verification Protocol





Run the complete three-step verification protocol on a real analysis.
First, sanity-check: open the Superstore data in your coding agent and ask three questions where you know the answer (row count, total revenue, number of unique customers). Does AI match?
Second, inspect methodology: for three queries, ask “show me the code.” Read each script. Is the logic correct? Then rephrase each question and run it again. Do the answers agree?
Third, spot-check edge cases: test three edge cases (empty results, boundary dates, null values). Does AI handle them gracefully?









Exercise 2: Red-Team Your Skill





Open your coding agent with the Chinook database and your SKILL.md from Module 4.
Run five adversarial queries designed to break the skill: a query about nonexistent data (“Sales in 2025”), a complex multi-table join (“Revenue by artist by country”), a request for data not in the schema (“Customer satisfaction scores”), an ambiguous column name, and a boundary date query.
For each failure, diagnose the root cause, fix the SKILL.md, and retest.









Exercise 3: RAG Citation Audit





Open your coding agent with the company handbook and ask five factual questions.
For each answer, locate every cited passage in the original document. Rate each citation as accurate, partially accurate, or hallucinated.
Then ask three questions the document cannot answer. Does AI admit it does not know, or does it hallucinate?









Exercise 4: Draft an AI Usage Policy





Write a one-page AI usage policy for your organization (or a hypothetical one).
Cover four areas. First, what data can and cannot be sent to AI providers, classified by sensitivity level. Second, which AI tools are approved for which use cases. Third, when human review is required before acting on AI output. Fourth, how RAG citations should be verified before sharing results externally.









Exercise 5: Compliance Scenario Analysis





Identify the primary data regulation in your industry (HIPAA, SOX, GDPR, PCI-DSS, FERPA, or another).
Describe a specific use case: what data would AI access, and what questions would it answer? Design the compliant workflow: what information goes to AI (schema only, or actual data)? What stays local? Where are the audit points?
List three guardrails you would add to enforce compliance.

title: 6  Reporting Pipelines
text:
6.1 From query to report
Module 1 introduced the agent loop — plan, execute, observe, iterate — and the concept of agents chaining tools together. This module puts that pattern to work. You will build a reporting pipeline that queries a database, generates charts, writes narrative, and assembles a PowerPoint deck, all from a single instruction.
In Module 4, the Chinook query app answered questions one at a time. You asked “What are the top ten countries by revenue?” and got a table. Useful, but a stakeholder does not want a table — they want a slide deck with a chart, key findings, and a recommendation.
Getting from a raw query to a finished report means executing a sequence of steps: run the SQL, transform the results, generate a visualization, write a summary, and assemble the output document. That sequence is a pipeline, and automating it is what agents do.

title: 6  Reporting Pipelines
text:
6.2 The reporting pipeline
Here is the workflow, step by step.
Step 1: Query the database. The agent runs a SQL query against the Chinook database — the same kind of query the Module 4 app generates. The result is a pandas DataFrame: rows of data ready for analysis.
Step 2: Render a chart. The agent passes the DataFrame to matplotlib (or Plotly) and generates a visualization — a bar chart, line chart, or whatever fits the data. The chart is saved as a PNG image.
Step 3: Generate a narrative. The agent sends the data to an LLM and asks for a concise executive summary. The prompt includes the numbers so the summary is grounded in facts, not hallucinated.
Step 4: Assemble the deck. The agent uses python-pptx to build a three-slide PowerPoint: a title slide with the report name and date, a slide with the chart, and a slide with the key findings. The deck is saved as a .pptx file.
Step 5: Deliver. In a Streamlit app, the chart and summary appear on screen, and a download button lets the user grab the PowerPoint file. The same pattern from Module 4 — Streamlit as the web wrapper — but the output is a document instead of a table.
Each step is straightforward. The value of the agent is chaining them together so the user clicks one button and gets a finished report.






flowchart LR
    Q["🗄️ Query DB\n_→ DataFrame_"]
    C["📊 Render Chart\n_→ PNG image_"]
    N["✍️ Write Narrative\n_→ executive summary_"]
    P["📑 Assemble Deck\n_→ .pptx file_"]
    D["🚀 Deliver\n_→ screen + download_"]

    Q --> C --> N --> P --> D

    style Q fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#2196f3
    style C fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#4caf50
    style N fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#ff9800
    style P fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#9c27b0
    style D fill:#fce4ec,stroke:#e91e63




Figure 6.1: The reporting pipeline — five steps from query to delivered report.

title: 6  Reporting Pipelines
section: 6.3 Building it with your coding agent
text:
6.3 Building it with your coding agent
You do not need to write this app by hand. Your coding agent can build it for you. Here is an example prompt you can give it:

Build a Streamlit app that connects to chinook.db. Add a dropdown with five pre-defined reports: Revenue by Country, Monthly Revenue Trend, Top 10 Artists, Genre Breakdown, and Customer Spending. When the user selects a report and clicks Generate, run the SQL query, create a chart with matplotlib, call OpenRouter to write a three-sentence executive summary, and assemble a three-slide PowerPoint (title, chart, findings) using python-pptx. Show the chart and summary on screen with a download button for the PowerPoint file. Log every LLM request and response to llm_log.jsonl with timestamps.

This prompt is specific enough that a coding agent can execute it end to end. It names the database, the reports, the tools (matplotlib, python-pptx, OpenRouter), the output format, and the logging requirement.
Your job is not to write the code. Your job is to test the result, refine the prompt, and iterate until the output meets your standards. If the charts are ugly, tell the agent to improve the styling. If the summaries are too generic, tell it to include specific numbers. If a report query is wrong, describe the correct logic. Prompt engineering is the skill; the agent is the labor.
Notice the last line of the prompt: logging. Every LLM request and response is written to a JSONL file with timestamps. This creates an audit trail — you can see exactly what the LLM was asked and what it returned. Module 5 covers how these logs support verification and governance.

title: 6  Reporting Pipelines
text:
6.4 Sandboxed execution
When an agent runs code, you need to control where it runs. On your laptop during development, a coding agent runs in your environment with access to your files. That is fine for prototyping. In production, you want isolation.






Built-in sandboxing



Codex includes OS-level sandboxing by default — it runs generated code in an isolated environment even on your laptop. Claude Code and Gemini CLI rely on permission prompts to control what the agent can do, but do not sandbox execution. For production deployment, all three benefit from the container-based isolation described below.


A container (Docker is the standard) is a disposable, isolated computing environment. Think of it as a virtual computer that exists only for one task and is destroyed afterward. It can only access what you explicitly allow. A bug in the AI-generated code cannot affect your other files, your database, or your network.
The sandbox pattern for production: a user asks for a report through a web interface, the agent plans and orchestrates, code runs inside a sandboxed container, the container queries the database through a read-only connection, and the finished report flows back to the user. The user never touches the database. The agent never escapes the sandbox. This is defense in depth.






flowchart LR
    User["👤 User"]
    UI["🌐 Web UI"]
    Agent["🤖 Agent"]

    subgraph Sandbox["🔒 Sandboxed Container"]
        Code["🐍 Generated\nCode"]
    end

    DB["🗄️ Database\n_read-only_"]
    Report["📊 Report"]

    User --> UI --> Agent --> Code
    Code -- "read-only\nquery" --> DB
    DB -- "results" --> Code
    Code --> Report --> UI --> User

    style Sandbox fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#ff9800,stroke-width:2px
    style DB fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#4caf50




Figure 6.2: Production sandbox architecture — the agent runs in isolation, the database is read-only.

title: 6  Reporting Pipelines
text:
6.5 From prototype to production
The reporting app on your laptop is a prototype. Moving it to production adds infrastructure, not intelligence.
Production adds SSO authentication so only authorized users can generate reports, scheduled runs so weekly reports are generated automatically, logging so every query and LLM call is recorded for compliance, and read-only database credentials so the agent cannot modify data.
The reporting pipeline you prototyped is the same artifact that powers the production tool. IT wraps it in infrastructure; you provide the domain knowledge and the prompt.

title: 6  Reporting Pipelines
text:
6.6 Exercises






Exercise 1: Multi-Step Agent Workflow





Open your coding agent in a folder containing the Chinook database and your SKILL.md from Module 4. Give it a single compound instruction that requires at least three steps.
For example: “Query the Chinook database for the top ten artists by revenue. Create a bar chart. Write a one-paragraph executive summary. Save the chart and summary to a reports folder.”
Observe the agent loop. How many distinct tool calls does the agent make? Does it check its own work? If the output is not right, refine your prompt and try again.









Exercise 2: Build the Report App





Use the example prompt from the “Building it with your coding agent” section to build the reporting app. Give the prompt to your coding agent and let it generate the app.
Test all five reports. For each, verify that the SQL returns correct data, the chart matches the data, the executive summary references actual numbers, and the PowerPoint has three slides.
If anything is wrong, refine your prompt and regenerate. Keep notes on what you changed and why.









Exercise 3: Workflow Decomposition





Consider the task: “Prepare the quarterly business review for the CEO.”
List the five to seven steps a human analyst would take to complete this task. For each step, identify what tool the agent would use and what data it would need. Mark which steps need human approval before the agent continues.
Then write a single prompt that describes the full workflow for an agent.









Exercise 4: Report App for Your Own Data





Adapt the reporting pattern to a dataset from your work. Pick a database, spreadsheet, or API that you query regularly.
Write an agent prompt that generates a report from your data — including the SQL (or data-fetching logic), chart type, summary style, and output format. Give the prompt to your coding agent and test the result.
If you cannot use real work data, use a public dataset: the Northwind database, NYC taxi data, or any CSV from your field.

title: 7  Agent Workflows
text:
7.1 Beyond reporting
Module 6 showed agents automating a reporting pipeline — a linear sequence from query to finished deck. But agents can orchestrate far more than reports. This module explores workflows where the agent coordinates across multiple systems — email, calendars, and scheduling — turning unstructured inputs into organized actions.
The reporting pipeline in Module 6 follows a predictable path: query data, make a chart, write a summary, assemble a document. The steps are well-defined and the output is a file.
Many real-world workflows are messier. A manager wants to schedule a team meeting but availability is scattered across email threads. A sales rep needs to draft follow-up emails based on notes from a dozen calls. An executive assistant needs to reorganize next week’s calendar after a last-minute conflict.
These tasks are multi-step, repetitive, and involve reasoning over unstructured text — exactly what agents do well.

title: 7  Agent Workflows
text:
7.2 Email agents
An email agent connects to your email account and performs tasks that would otherwise require you to read, sort, and respond to messages manually.
Search and summarize. Instead of scrolling through your inbox, you ask the agent: “What emails came in about the Q2 budget this week?” The agent searches your email, retrieves the relevant messages, and returns a summary with key points from each.
Draft responses. You tell the agent: “Draft a reply to Sarah’s email accepting the meeting time but asking to move it to the conference room.” The agent reads the original message, drafts an appropriate reply, and presents it for your review before sending.
Organize and triage. The agent scans incoming messages and groups them: urgent items needing a response today, FYI messages that can wait, and newsletters to archive. Instead of processing 50 emails one by one, you review three categories.
The pattern is the same as the reporting pipeline: the agent chains tools together (search email, read message, generate text, send reply) in a loop. The difference is that the data source is your inbox instead of a database.






flowchart LR
    T["📝 Task\n_user instruction_"]
    S["🔍 Search\n_find messages_"]
    R["🧠 Reason\n_extract key info_"]
    D["✍️ Draft\n_compose response_"]
    V["👤 Review\n_human approval_"]

    T --> S --> R --> D --> V

    style T fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#2196f3
    style S fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#4caf50
    style R fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#ff9800
    style D fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#9c27b0
    style V fill:#fce4ec,stroke:#e91e63




Figure 7.1: An email agent workflow — search, reason, draft, and deliver.

title: 7  Agent Workflows
text:
7.3 Calendar agents
A calendar agent reads and writes events on your calendar. It can answer questions (“When is my next meeting with the marketing team?”), find open slots (“What times am I free on Thursday afternoon?”), and create or move events.
The real power emerges when the calendar agent works with other tools. Combined with email, it can read a message proposing a meeting time, check your calendar for conflicts, and draft a reply confirming or suggesting alternatives — all from a single instruction.
Calendar agents also illustrate a key agent design principle: human-in-the-loop. Reading your calendar is low-risk; the agent should do it freely. Creating or moving events changes your schedule and affects other people. A well-designed calendar agent proposes changes and waits for your approval before executing them.

title: 7  Agent Workflows
text:
7.4 Using AI for scheduling
Scheduling is a constraint satisfaction problem. You have a set of people, each with their own availability, and you need to find a time that works for everyone. Traditionally, this means sending a poll, chasing responses, and manually comparing calendars.
An agent can do this conversationally. Here is the workflow:

Gather constraints. The agent reads email threads where participants mentioned their availability: “I’m free Tuesday afternoon,” “Wednesday works except before 10,” “Any day but Friday.” These constraints are scattered across unstructured text — exactly the kind of input that LLMs excel at parsing.
Check calendars. The agent looks at each participant’s calendar to find existing commitments. It combines the stated preferences from email with the hard conflicts from calendars.
Propose options. The agent reasons through the constraints and proposes two or three time slots that work for everyone (or the largest subset, if no time works for all).
Confirm and book. After the organizer approves, the agent sends calendar invitations and a confirmation email.







flowchart LR
    E["📧 Read Emails\n_extract preferences_"]
    C["📅 Check Calendars\n_find conflicts_"]
    P["🧠 Propose Slots\n_satisfy constraints_"]
    A["👤 Approve\n_human decision_"]
    B["✅ Book & Notify\n_send invites_"]

    E --> C --> P --> A --> B

    style E fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#2196f3
    style C fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#4caf50
    style P fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#ff9800
    style A fill:#fce4ec,stroke:#e91e63
    style B fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#9c27b0




Figure 7.2: Scheduling as an agent workflow — from scattered constraints to a booked meeting.





This is fundamentally different from a scheduling poll. A poll collects structured yes/no responses. An agent reads natural language, infers availability from context, and handles ambiguity (“I’d prefer mornings” is a soft constraint, not a hard one). The agent is doing what a skilled executive assistant does — reasoning through messy, human communication to find a workable solution.

title: 7  Agent Workflows
text:
7.5 Document processing agents
The scheduling example coordinates across calendars and email. Another powerful pattern coordinates across file formats. In many business workflows, the information you need is scattered across Excel spreadsheets, PDF reports, Word memos, and CSV exports — each in a different structure with different conventions.
A document processing agent ingests all of these, extracts the relevant information from each, and combines the results into a single analysis. The pattern looks like this:






flowchart LR
    I["📂 Ingest\n_multiple formats_"]
    X["🔍 Extract\n_rules & data_"]
    A["🧠 Analyze\n_apply & combine_"]
    O["📄 Output\n_report or memo_"]

    I --> X --> A --> O

    style I fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#2196f3
    style X fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#4caf50
    style A fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#ff9800
    style O fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#9c27b0




Figure 7.3: Document processing agent — ingest multiple formats, extract, analyze, and deliver.





Here are three examples of this pattern in finance and business:
M&A due diligence. An acquisition target sends a data room with a customer list (Excel), contract terms (PDF), the acquirer’s screening criteria (Word), and industry benchmarks (CSV). You tell the agent: “Evaluate this acquisition target.” It reads each file, extracts contract terms and change-of-control provisions from the PDF, applies the screening criteria to each customer, benchmarks against industry standards, and produces a due diligence memo suitable for the investment committee.
Loan portfolio risk analysis. A bank’s risk team has a loan tape with 220 loans (Excel), state banking regulations (PDF), and an internal risk policy memo (Word). The agent extracts regulatory thresholds from the PDF, pulls classification criteria from the Word document, assigns each loan a risk tier, flags regulatory violations, and produces a risk report with tier distribution and estimated reserves.
Portfolio consolidation. An investor holds positions across three brokerages, each reporting in a different format (two Excel workbooks and a CSV). The agent reads all three, standardizes the columns, resolves ticker symbol inconsistencies, sums positions that appear in multiple accounts, and produces a single consolidated view with total portfolio value and asset allocation.
The key insight is that these tasks are not difficult for a human — they are tedious. Reading a 30-page PDF to find three regulatory thresholds, then cross-referencing them against 220 rows in a spreadsheet, is exactly the kind of work that agents eliminate. The agent reads every format natively, never loses track of which rule came from which document, and finishes in minutes rather than hours.

title: 7  Agent Workflows
text:
7.6 Variance analysis agents
Variance analysis — comparing budgeted figures to actual results and decomposing the differences — is the core analytical task in FP&A. Every public company does it quarterly, and boards and investors ask “why did we miss?” after every earnings period.
An agent can perform a full variance decomposition from a single prompt. Consider this scenario:

Q1 actuals just closed. Operating income missed budget by $300K.

Revenue: Budget 100K units at $50 = $5M; Actual 95K units at $51 = $4.845M — $155K miss
COGS: Budget $30/unit; Actual $32/unit — $40K miss
SG&A: Budget $1.45M; Actual $1.555M — $105K miss


You prompt the agent: “Here is our Q1 budget vs. actuals spreadsheet. Decompose the $300K operating income miss into volume, price, cost, and discretionary spending drivers. Produce a waterfall chart and a summary memo for the CFO.”
The agent plans the decomposition, separates revenue variance into volume and price effects, breaks COGS variance into volume-driven and rate-driven components, itemizes SG&A overruns, generates a waterfall chart showing how each driver contributed to the $300K gap, and writes a CFO-ready memo — all from that single instruction.
This replaces two to four hours of manual FP&A work. The agent applies the same variance decomposition formulas an analyst would, but it does so instantly and presents the results in a format ready for executive review.

title: 7  Agent Workflows
text:
7.7 The MCP connection
These workflows become practical through MCP (Model Context Protocol), which you met in Module 4. Just as an MCP connector gave your agent access to a database, connectors exist for Gmail, Google Calendar, Outlook, Slack, and dozens of other services.
The pattern is consistent: install a connector, grant the agent access, and it gains new tools. A Gmail connector gives the agent search_email, read_message, and send_email tools. A Google Calendar connector gives it list_events, create_event, and find_free_time tools. The agent chains these tools the same way it chained SQL queries and chart generation in Module 6.

title: 7  Agent Workflows
text:
7.8 Designing safe agent workflows
As agents gain access to more systems, safety becomes more important. The principles from Module 5 apply here, plus a few specific to multi-system workflows.
Least privilege. Give the agent only the permissions it needs. An email agent that summarizes your inbox needs read access, not send access. A calendar agent that checks availability needs read access, not the ability to delete events.
Human approval for actions. Reading data is low-risk. Writing data (sending an email, booking a meeting, modifying a document) is high-risk. Design workflows so the agent proposes actions and a human approves them before execution.
Audit trails. Log every action the agent takes across every system. When the agent reads an email, searches a calendar, and drafts a response, each step should be recorded. If something goes wrong, you can trace exactly what happened.

title: 7  Agent Workflows
text:
7.9 Exercises






Exercise 1: Agent Use Case Inventory





Review your team’s recurring tasks and identify five that are multi-step, repetitive, and data-driven — good candidates for agent automation.
For each, describe the trigger (what starts the task), the steps (what happens), the output (what gets delivered), and the frequency (how often it runs). Rate each on automation potential (high, medium, or low) and risk level (high, medium, or low).
Pick the highest-potential, lowest-risk workflow as your pilot candidate and write one paragraph explaining why you would start there.









Exercise 2: Email Workflow Design





Pick one email-related task you do regularly (triaging your inbox, drafting status updates, summarizing threads for your manager, etc.).
Write a detailed prompt that describes the workflow step by step. Specify what the agent should search for, what information it should extract, what it should draft, and what requires your approval before sending.
If you have a coding agent with an email connector available, test the workflow. If not, describe the workflow precisely enough that a colleague could execute it manually.









Exercise 3: Scheduling Scenario





Imagine you need to schedule a one-hour meeting with four colleagues. Write out the constraints for each person in natural language (as they might appear in email):

Person A: “I’m pretty open this week, but I have a dentist appointment Wednesday morning.”
Person B: “Tuesday and Thursday work for me, preferably after lunch.”
Person C: “I’m slammed Monday and Friday. Any other day before 3 PM.”
Person D: “I can do Wednesday or Thursday, but not before 11.”

Work through the constraints manually and find all valid one-hour slots. Then describe how you would prompt an agent to solve this — what information would you provide, and what output would you expect?









Exercise 4: Permission Design





For each of the following agent capabilities, decide whether it should be automatic (no approval needed), require human approval, or be prohibited entirely:

Reading your email inbox
Sending an email on your behalf
Reading your calendar
Creating a calendar event
Deleting a calendar event
Forwarding an email to someone outside your organization
Accessing a shared team calendar

For each decision, write one sentence explaining your reasoning. Compare your answers with a colleague — where do you disagree, and why?









Exercise 5: Document Processing Workflow





Give your coding agent a folder containing files in at least two different formats (for example, an Excel spreadsheet and a PDF, or a CSV and a Word document). Ask the agent to extract information from each file, combine the results, and produce a summary report.
Some ideas to get started:

M&A due diligence: a customer list (Excel), contract terms (PDF), screening criteria (Word), and industry benchmarks (CSV) — ask the agent to evaluate the target and produce a memo.
Loan risk analysis: a loan tape (Excel), banking regulations (PDF), and a risk policy (Word) — ask the agent to classify loans and flag violations.
Any multi-source analysis from your work: combine data from different systems or departments that arrive in different formats.

Pay attention to how the agent handles each format. Does it correctly extract the relevant information? Does it combine the sources logically? What would you need to verify before trusting the output?

title: 8  Retrieval-Augmented Generation
text:
8.1 The problem: AI does not know your documents
The previous modules worked with structured data — spreadsheets and databases where every row has the same columns. But much of the knowledge in an organization lives in unstructured documents: policy handbooks, contracts, product manuals, research reports, meeting transcripts. This module covers Retrieval-Augmented Generation, or RAG — the technique that lets AI answer questions about your documents with grounded, cited answers instead of hallucinations.
AI models are trained on public data. They know a great deal about the world in general, but they know nothing about your company’s travel policy, your product warranty terms, or last quarter’s board report.
Without RAG, if you ask “What is our return policy for enterprise customers?” the LLM will either make something up (confidently and plausibly) or admit it does not know. Neither response is useful. What you want is for the system to look up the answer in your actual policy document, quote the relevant passage, and tell you where it found it.
That is what RAG does.
RAG is one of several ways to give AI knowledge it was not trained on. The choice depends on how much control you need and how much effort you can invest.
flowchart TD
    subgraph RAG["📄 RAG"]
        R1["Feed documents at query time"]
        R2["Best when facts change often\nor data is proprietary"]
        R3["_Least effort — no model changes_"]
    end
    subgraph FT["🔧 Fine-Tuning"]
        F1["Retrain the model on your data"]
        F2["Best for specialized tone,\nformat, or domain expertise"]
        F3["_Moderate effort — needs training data_"]
    end
    subgraph SLM["🏠 Small Language Model"]
        S1["Run a private model on your hardware"]
        S2["Best for full control,\nprivacy, or niche tasks"]
        S3["_Most effort — full infrastructure_"]
    end

    RAG --- FT --- SLM

    style RAG fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#4caf50
    style FT fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#ff9800
    style SLM fill:#fce4ec,stroke:#e91e63




Figure 8.1: Three ways to give AI knowledge it was not trained on — from least effort to most control.
This module focuses on RAG — the lightest-weight option and the one most accessible to business teams without machine-learning infrastructure.

title: 8  Retrieval-Augmented Generation
text:
8.2 The open-book exam analogy
The simplest way to understand RAG is the open-book exam. Standard AI is a closed-book exam: the student answers from memory, is confident even when wrong, and cannot cite sources. RAG is an open-book exam: the student looks up the answer in the reference materials, quotes the source, and can say “this is not covered in my materials” when the answer is not there.
The quality of the answer depends entirely on what is in the book. If the documents are incomplete, outdated, or poorly organized, the RAG system will produce incomplete, outdated, or confused answers. The AI is only as good as the documents you give it.

title: 8  Retrieval-Augmented Generation
text:
8.3 The RAG pipeline
RAG works in three stages: ingest, retrieve, and generate.
In the ingest stage, documents are split into chunks — typically paragraphs or sections — and each chunk is converted into a numerical representation called an embedding. The embeddings are stored in a vector database (a searchable index optimized for similarity search).
In the retrieve stage, when a user asks a question, the question is also converted into an embedding. The system finds the chunks whose embeddings are most similar to the question’s embedding. These are the chunks most likely to contain the answer.
In the generate stage, the retrieved chunks are fed to the LLM along with the user’s question. The LLM reads the chunks and generates an answer, citing the sources.






flowchart LR

    end
    subgraph Retrieve["2. Retrieve"]
        direction TB
        Question["❓ Question"] --> Embed2["🔢 Embed"] --> Search["🔍 Similarity\nSearch"] --> Chunks["📋 Relevant\nChunks"]
    end

title: 8  Retrieval-Augmented Generation
text:
8.4 Embeddings and semantic search
Embeddings are what make RAG fundamentally different from keyword search.
Traditional keyword search finds documents containing the exact words you typed. If you search for “vacation policy,” you will find documents containing those words but miss documents that say “PTO guidelines” or “time-off rules” or “leave entitlement.”
Semantic search works on meaning. Embeddings convert text into high-dimensional numerical vectors such that texts with similar meanings point in similar directions. When you search for “vacation policy,” semantic search finds all of those variations because they have similar embeddings. It understands meaning, not just words.
This is why RAG can answer questions in natural language. You do not need to know the exact terminology used in the document. You ask the question in your own words and the system finds the relevant passages regardless of how they are phrased.






Figure 8.3: Keyword search finds only exact word matches. Semantic search finds all passages with the same meaning — even when the wording differs.

title: 8  Retrieval-Augmented Generation
text:
8.5 Citations and source attribution
RAG does not just answer — it shows where the answer came from.
Each answer includes references to the source documents and, when possible, page numbers or section headings. Users can click through to verify the original text. If the answer is not in the documents, a well-built RAG system says so instead of hallucinating.
Citations transform AI from “trust me” to “here’s the evidence.” This is what makes RAG suitable for high-stakes use cases where accuracy matters and where someone will eventually ask “where did you get that number?”

title: 8  Retrieval-Augmented Generation
text:
8.6 When to use RAG
RAG is not always the right approach. The choice depends on the type of data.
For structured data (spreadsheets, databases, tables of numbers), a skill that writes SQL queries is more appropriate. This is what you built in Module 4.
For unstructured documents (policies, contracts, manuals, reports), RAG is the right tool.
For small texts that fit within the AI’s context window (fewer than about 100 pages), you can skip the RAG pipeline entirely and just give the document directly to the AI. A coding agent does this naturally when you point it at a folder containing documents.






Context window sizes



The amount of text an agent can process in one session varies. Gemini CLI supports up to one million tokens (roughly 3,000 pages). Codex supports 192,000 tokens. Claude Code’s limit depends on the model, ranging from 200,000 to over 1,000,000 tokens. For most business documents, any of these is sufficient for direct reading without RAG.


For situations where you need both structured queries and document context, you can combine a skill with RAG — querying the database for numbers and the documents for context.

title: 8  Retrieval-Augmented Generation
text:
8.7 Why chunk size matters
Documents are split into chunks before embedding. The size of these chunks determines what the system can find and what it misses.
Chunks that are too small (individual sentences) lose context. A sentence alone may be meaningless without the paragraph around it. The system retrieves fragments instead of answers.
Chunks that are too large (entire chapters) include too much irrelevant content. The signal is diluted by noise. The AI may struggle to identify which part of the chunk actually answers the question.
The sweet spot is usually a few paragraphs — enough context to be meaningful, small enough to be relevant. Overlapping chunks (where each chunk shares some text with its neighbors) help ensure that nothing falls through the cracks.

title: 8  Retrieval-Augmented Generation
text:
8.8 Common failure modes
RAG is not perfect. Understanding the failure modes helps you spot and fix problems.
On the retrieval side, the system may retrieve the wrong chunk (semantically similar but irrelevant), miss the right chunk entirely (the answer exists but was not retrieved), or retrieve chunks that contradict each other.
On the generation side, the LLM may hallucinate a citation (citing a page that does not contain the claim), over-generalize (drawing a conclusion the source does not support), or give an incomplete answer (using one chunk when the full answer spans multiple documents).
The most dangerous failure is the hallucinated citation. The answer looks grounded — it has a source reference — but when you check the cited passage, it does not say what the LLM claims. This creates false confidence, which is worse than no citation at all.

title: 8  Retrieval-Augmented Generation
text:
8.9 Hands-on: querying your documents
The simplest way to experience RAG is to point your coding agent at a folder of documents and start asking questions.
Download the sample company handbook and place it in a folder. Open your coding agent in that folder. Then ask questions:

What is our return policy for enterprise customers?


What are the eligibility requirements for the bonus program?


How many days of PTO do employees with five years of service receive?

For each answer, evaluate three things. Is it relevant — did it find the right section of the document? Is it faithful — does the answer accurately represent what the source says? Is it complete — did it find all the relevant passages, or did it miss something?
Then test the boundaries. Ask a question the document cannot answer: “What is our competitor’s market share?” Does AI admit the information is not in the document, or does it hallucinate an answer?

title: 8  Retrieval-Augmented Generation
text:
8.10 Evaluating RAG quality
There are three dimensions for judging whether a RAG answer is good.
Relevance measures whether the retrieved passages are on-topic. Did the system find the right sections? Would a human reading the document have picked the same passages?
Faithfulness measures whether the generated answer accurately represents the source material. Does the answer match what the cited passage actually says? Are there any added claims that go beyond the source?
Completeness measures whether the system found everything relevant. Did it retrieve all the passages that bear on the question? Would adding more context from the document change the answer?
Use these three questions to evaluate every RAG answer: is it relevant, faithful, and complete?

title: 8  Retrieval-Augmented Generation
text:
8.11 RAG in production
In production, RAG has a document pipeline that runs continuously. New documents are automatically chunked and embedded. A vector store (Pinecone, Weaviate, Chroma, or similar) maintains a searchable index of all document chunks. When a user asks a question, the system retrieves the most relevant chunks, feeds them to the AI, and returns a cited answer.
Production RAG requires ongoing maintenance. Documents need refresh schedules so that updated policies replace outdated ones. Access controls ensure that users can only query documents they are authorized to see. Versioning tracks changes so that when a policy is updated, the old version expires from the index.
Quality assurance means maintaining a test suite of known question-answer pairs to validate accuracy, monitoring retrieval quality over time, and building a feedback loop where users can flag incorrect answers for improvement.
The same sandbox-audit-deploy pattern from Module 5 applies. RAG is a system, not a feature, and it needs the same care as any production system.

title: 8  Retrieval-Augmented Generation
text:
8.12 Exercises






Exercise 1: Document Querying





Download the company handbook and place it in a folder. Open your coding agent in that folder and ask five questions about the content.
For each answer, evaluate relevance (did it find the right section?), faithfulness (does the answer match the source?), and completeness (did it find everything relevant?). Rate each dimension on a 1-to-5 scale.
Then ask two questions the document cannot answer. Does AI admit it does not know, or does it hallucinate?









Exercise 2: Citation Verification





Ask three questions about the company handbook and get answers with citations.
For each answer, locate every cited passage in the original document. Rate each citation: accurate (the passage says what the AI claims), partially accurate (the passage is related but the AI stretched or paraphrased), or hallucinated (the passage does not support the claim).
If any citation is inaccurate, rephrase the question and see if the citation improves.









Exercise 3: Conflicting Sources





Create a second version of the company handbook in which you change one or two facts (for example, change the PTO accrual rates or the return policy window). Place both versions in the same folder.
Ask a question where the two versions disagree. Does AI cite both? Pick one? Acknowledge the conflict?
This simulates a common production problem: stale documents coexisting with current ones.









Exercise 4: RAG vs. Direct Reading





Ask your coding agent to read the entire company handbook as one document and answer a question (this is the direct-context approach). Then ask the same question in a way that forces chunked retrieval (for example, by placing many documents in the folder so the handbook is too large for the context window to hold in full).
Compare the two answers. Which is more complete? Does the direct-reading approach catch context that chunked retrieval misses?
This exercise illustrates the tradeoff: direct context is better for small document sets, while RAG scales to thousands of documents.









Exercise 5: RAG Use Case Proposal





Write a one-page proposal for deploying RAG in your organization.
Identify the document set (employee handbook, product documentation, compliance policies, or whatever is most relevant to your work). Describe the users and their typical questions. Outline the architecture: how documents would be ingested, where the vector store would live, and how users would interact with the system.
List three risks and how you would mitigate them. The most common risks are hallucinated citations (mitigated by citation verification workflows), stale documents (mitigated by refresh schedules), and unauthorized access (mitigated by document-level permissions).

title: Alternative Tools
text:
Alternative coding agents
This course uses Claude Code via the Claude Desktop app, but the concepts and workflows apply to other coding agents and interfaces. This appendix describes the alternatives.
Claude Code is also available as a standalone terminal tool for users who prefer a command-line interface. Google and OpenAI offer similar terminal-based coding agents:
Gemini CLI is Google’s equivalent. It requires Node.js version 18 or higher and is installed with npm install -g @google/gemini-cli. It offers a free tier with 60 requests per minute and 1,000 requests per day. It reads a GEMINI.md file for project-level instructions, serving the same purpose as Claude Code’s CLAUDE.md.
OpenAI Codex is an open-source terminal agent that connects to OpenAI’s models. It is installed with npm install -g @openai/codex (requires Node.js 22 or higher). OpenAI also offers a native desktop app at openai.com/codex. Codex requires a ChatGPT Plus subscription or an OpenAI API key. It reads project instructions from an AGENTS.md file.
All three agents read local files, write Python, and return results in conversation. The workflows in this course apply to any of them. The key differences are cost (Claude Code requires a $20/month subscription or API credits; Gemini CLI’s free tier covers most individual use; Codex is open source but OpenAI API calls are billed per token), context windows (Gemini supports up to one million tokens; Claude Code ranges from 200,000 to over one million; Codex supports 192,000), and sandboxing (Codex uses an OS-level sandbox by default; Claude Code and Gemini CLI rely on permission prompts).

title: Alternative Tools
text:
Other interfaces
The Chat and Cowork tabs in Claude Desktop serve different purposes from the Code tab. Chat is a standard conversation interface for questions, writing, and image analysis — no code execution. Cowork runs tasks in a sandboxed virtual machine: it can organize files, draft reports, and process documents, but in a more isolated environment than the Code tab. Cowork is useful for batch knowledge work where you want an extra layer of safety.
Google Antigravity is an agent-first IDE — a fork of VS Code designed around AI collaboration. It is currently in free preview. Rather than adding AI to an existing editor, it was built from the ground up for agent-driven workflows, with the agent able to navigate files, run code, and iterate on changes directly in the editor.
VS Code with AI extensions is the most common setup for developers who already use VS Code. Claude Code, Gemini CLI, and Codex all have VS Code extensions that embed the agent directly in the editor.
